Successfully polled for done (polled for)=0.000020 seconds -- Device Path: /sv/arden-07/vm0 B:D.F: c9:00.00 -- [INFO] Entering Polling Loop for checkDone [INFO] Successfully polled for done (polled for)=0.000020 seconds  22611598062 qata Seems like all of the VM's did actually start and were attempted to be stopped by Supercollider. Because we can see the MEM_WR's to start the VM's and then ~1.5M cycles later we see the writes to stop the VM's.  What is noticeable is that the threads that have the TIMEOUT are the ones where Supercollider had to do a bit of polling yet at the end it still sees the VM being active.  I'm wondering if it's just taking some extra time to drain out and get the replies for the P2P accesses.  Will do another test, where we run a script right after Supercollider finishes and just continues to poll the VM statuses to see if it ever finishes. Will go ahead and also write the enable bit back to the VM's because Supercollider sets it to disable at the end.  -------- I also don't see any arguments to Supercollider where I can modify the amount of time it is polling the VTC VM statuses before it determines its TIMEOUT. Its just a library call from the ArdenAPI.  22611603982 qata In the case of the DDIO test - /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/mcp_multistack/manual/ddio/22020670198_hiop_ddio_lock_fetchadd.0  - It seems like it might be a different issue altogether - might be a VTC problem because it doesn't seem like this is correct where the TAG is being reused between the CAS and the MEM_WR  8521375312 U         CAS --- ---- 252 5900 0000 ---- 0 ---- 00000005 00500030 ---- - 0 0 0 --------------- 0 0     8 6E000008 59000000 00000005 00500030 0 -------- -------- - 00 00 --- -- ---- ----  --------- - ----------- --- ---- --- ---- ---- ---- - ---- -------- -------- ---- - - - - --------------- - -     0 00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000 - -------- -------- - -- -- --- -- ---- ----  --------- - ----------- --- ---- --- ---- ---- ---- - ---- -------- -------- ---- - - - - --------------- - -     4 02000000 00000000 00000000 00000000 - -------- -------- - -- -- --- -- ---- ----  --------- - ----------- --- ---- --- ---- ---- ---- - ---- -------- -------- ---- - - - -              H0 - - ----- 6E000008 59000000 00000005 00500030 - -------- -------- - -- -- --- -- ---- ----  8521375472 U      MEM_WR --- ---- 253 5900 0000 ---- 0 ---- 00000005 00500070 ---- - 0 0 0 --------------- 0 0     4 60000004 59000000 00000005 00500070 0 -------- -------- - 00 00 --- -- ---- ----  --------- - ----------- --- ---- --- ---- ---- ---- - ---- -------- -------- ---- - - - - --------------- - -     0 38003940 3A003B40 3C003D40 3E003F40 - -------- -------- - -- -- --- -- ---- ----  --------- - ----------- --- ---- --- ---- ---- ---- - ---- -------- -------- ---- - - - -              H0 - - ----- 60000004 59000000 00000005 00500070 - -------- -------- - -- -- --- -- ---- ---- CAS is considered an Atomic (part of the test for DDIO here) and all Atomics are supposed to be non-posted, which all require completions before reusing the TAG. Will probably try and reproduce this in simics, but likely this one might just go to the VTC team to take a look at.  We'll continue to debug the TIMEOUT issue when that run finishes.  22611677441 suratako @Ta, Quang A Please update the next steps on this PS. Has this been escalated to a different val tenant, no links other than TR's?  22611679762 qata After writing the script that parses through the pcie logs for analysis, it doesn't seem like there's anything pending by the end of the test. So there shouldn't really be any reason why the VMMR's would still indicate that the VM's are running. There's something maybe internally... or when Supercollider tries to clear the VM enable bit after it fails the timeout that messes up the VM statuses at the end of the test. Since the pythonsv polling scripts didn't work and it looked like the statuses all said that the VM's were still running at the end of the test.  I think for now... we have to treat this as a special case because we can't change supercollider code itself to not try to disable the VM's if it times out. We'll have an identifier in the tool to and will probably add a signature to kokua to identify this with no real possible solutions at this time. We don't have a good way to extend Supercollider's polling time right now and we'd need to file an enhancement ticket either to Supercollider team or the pcietc library team to allow Supercollider to give us some option to increase the poll time in these cases or some option where Supercollider will not try to disable the VM's if it timeout so that we can check the final status of the VM's at the end of the run without something possibly bad being done in the middle.  So really... I think the enhancement request we need to make to Supercollider team is: 1. Provide some option where - if supercollider see's a pcietc timeout when polling for the vm status -> it does not go and try to set the vmx_enable=0 or any other bit in the vm_mode register and instead just continue on with the rest of supercollider cleanup.      - This is to allow users to be able to go back in later in the case of slow progress p2p commands to check if eventually the VM status goes to stop during infinite mode.  22611679987 qata Actually... I think the status of the VM is contingent on the fact that all of the vectors finish for a given loop. This is seen in the single passing case for PRd in the run -  /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/mcp_multistack/manual/cross_imh/22020670195_hiop_p2p_cross_imh_base.3 Only arden-01, 05, 07 hit the PCIETC Timeout. While arden-03 (which is the only PRd that didn't hit the PCIETC TIMEOUT) finishes. When you compare a passing PRd thread and one of the failing PRd threads it's actually quite obvious something happened on the VM side to where it just stopped sending out MEM_RD's even though CMPLT_D's are returning later. So then it will never set the VM end status to be done. I think maybe actually we should bring this up with VTC team as a potential issue that maybe just warrants more investigation on the VTC side asking why doesn't the failing VM's finish its vector loop in order to set the end status eventually.  ^[[u^[[0J^[[44;1mSUPERCOLLIDER:^[[0m Ways=4   Sets=64     DfltAddrAlgo=DFT                   Time=000:00:30^M ^M Thrd    Type    Ways  Sets Tgt Of Attr    Instr     Agent                     Loops^M ---- ---------- ---- ----- --- -- ---- ----------- ------------------------ ----------^M    0       Idle    4    64   0  0   WB     default S-1 C-1 T-1   pid=   2404     2671459^M    1        PWr    4    63   5 28      /sv/arden-00/vm0^M    2        PRd    4    63   6 23      /sv/arden-01/vm0^M    3        PWr    4    63   7 26      /sv/arden-02/vm0^M    4        PRd    4    63   8 15      /sv/arden-03/vm0^M    5        PWr    4    63   1 41      /sv/arden-04/vm0^M    6        PRd    4    63   2 37      /sv/arden-05/vm0^M    7        PWr    4    63   3 54      /sv/arden-06/vm0^M    8        PRd    4    63   4 30      /sv/arden-07/vm0^M ^M Note: Updater=t0. 0 0 0 --------------- F 0     1 60010001 58000000 00001220 C50E5000 0 -------- -------- - 01 00 --- -- ---- ----  - In between this is just a whole bunch of CMPLT_D returning for all of the outstanding MEM_RD's What we need help from VTC team is to understand why doesn't the VM finish the rest of the vectors it was programmed to do so?  22611679991 qata I think we'll promote this to sighting and then we'll want to push to VTC database to see if they can help answer why the VM isn't continuing with the programmed vectors.

raw.title: [DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][SVOS] OS reporting different memory size/structure after reset
raw.description: Ran 1st cold boot and 2nd cold boot tests and the OS is reporting different memory size/structure, even though the OS and BIOS are the same between the two tests.  Also seeing different memory size/structure for WR and CR tests, where the boot and reset are reporting different memory size/structure. 1st Cold Boot: [    1.786433] Memory: 16452752K/16916016K available (13517K kernel code, 2439K rwdata, 9812K rodata, 3516K init, 4140K bss, 429616K reserved , 0K cma-reserved) 2nd Cold Boot (bad CMOS): [    1.517471] Memory: 16421404K/16880536K available (13517K kernel code, 2439K rwdata, 9812K rodata, 3516K init, 4140K bss, 425484K reserved , 0K cma-reserved) 2nd Cold Boot (RTC restore and CMOS restore was correct): [    1.517374] Memory: 16421448K/16880580K available (13517K kernel code, 2439K rwdata, 9812K rodata, 3516K init, 4140K bss, 425484K reserved , 0K cma-reserved) Cold Reset: Boot- [    1.907804] Memory: 16456528K/16916492K available (13517K kernel code, 2439K rwdata, 9812K rodata, 3516K init, 4140K bss, 429668K reserved , 0K cma-reserved) Reset- [    1.600415] Memory: 16423912K/16880484K available (13517K kernel code, 2439K rwdata, 9812K rodata, 3516K init, 4140K bss, 425520K reserved , 0K cma-reserved) Warm Reset: Boot- [    1.849277] Memory: 16456068K/16916020K available (13517K kernel code, 2439K rwdata, 9812K rodata, 3516K init, 4140K bss, 429656K reserved, 0K cma-reserved) Reset- [    1.591730] Memory: 16423976K/16880544K available (13517K kernel code, 2439K rwdata, 9812K rodata, 3516K init, 4140K bss, 425516K reserved , 0K cma-reserved) UART logs for these test runs are attached to this record.

raw.comments: 22611599840 eeparks 14024874521 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611599841 eeparks 14024874522 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611599844 eeparks 22020413864 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611599845 eeparks 22020413876 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611726936 eeparks Hello margarita,   As far as SVOS is concerned, it will attempt to take any usable memory advertised to us through the e820 tables and divy it up based on certain parameters. First, it will attempt to take all low memory(memory below 4GB) that is marked as usable and manually set those to reserved for use for svos memory targets later. After the 4GB range, usable memory will first be allocated to the kernel memory that is assigned by the svos=mem@ parameter. In this instance the amount is listed in your output from the memory available section. After memory is set aside fro the kernel, the remaining usable memory is set to reserved for use for svos memory targets.   Regardless of cold/warm reboot this is what svos will do, so any deviation from this will occur due to changes in what is advertised to us from the e820 table. I would consider looking at the e820 table before and after the boot that shows you change in memory.   Thanks, Carlos   From: Olaya-cabrera, Margarita Sent: Tuesday, January 27, 2026 10:56 AM To: Garcia, Alfred C ; Molina Alvarez, Carlos ; Cervantes Dueas, Roberto Cc: Mayorga Macias, Walter A ; Hernandez Miramontes, Jose Miguel ; Parks, Edwin E ; Altamirano, Josh Subject: RE: Memory Size Variations for Boot and Reset   Roberto, Carlos,   Have you been able to review the data and determine next steps?   Regards, Margarita   From: Garcia, Alfred C Sent: Thursday, January 8, 2026 5:09 PM To: Olaya-cabrera, Margarita ; Molina Alvarez, Carlos ; Cervantes Dueas, Roberto Cc: Mayorga Macias, Walter A ; Hernandez Miramontes, Jose Miguel ; Parks, Edwin E ; Altamirano, Josh Subject: RE: Memory Size Variations for Boot and Reset   Hi All,   Here are the dmesg logs for first boot and after reset.   Thanks & Regards,   Alfred Garcia Pre-Si Valid/Verif Engineer (iVE PHG CED) Work/Cell: 619-884-1874 E-mail: alfred.c.garcia@intel.com “Knowledge is Power. Knowledge Shared is Power Multiplied.” - Robert Noyce.   From: Garcia, Alfred C Sent: Thursday, January 8, 2026 11:18 AM To: Olaya-cabrera, Margarita ; Molina Alvarez, Carlos ; Cervantes Dueas, Roberto Cc: Mayorga Macias, Walter A ; Hernandez Miramontes, Jose Miguel ; Parks, Edwin E ; Altamirano, Josh Subject: RE: Memory Size Variations for Boot and Reset   Hi Josh,   Can you share your run directory or your command line to reproduce the issue?   As a follow-up from Roberto, I plan on taking the dmesg output of the first boot and then dmesg of subsequent boots.   Thanks & Regards,   Alfred Garcia Pre-Si Valid/Verif Engineer (iVE PHG CED) Work/Cell: 619-884-1874 E-mail: alfred.c.garcia@intel.com “Knowledge is Power. Knowledge Shared is Power Multiplied.” - Robert Noyce.   From: Olaya-cabrera, Margarita Sent: Thursday, January 8, 2026 11:08 AM To: Molina Alvarez, Carlos ; Cervantes Dueas, Roberto Cc: Mayorga Macias, Walter A ; Hernandez Miramontes, Jose Miguel ; Parks, Edwin E ; Garcia, Alfred C ; Altamirano, Josh Subject: RE: Memory Size Variations for Boot and Reset   Hi Carlos,   Can help us with this open? The issue is that we are noticing a memory difference in used/available from first boot to SVOS vs subsequent boots when we do resets. Adding you since you are listed as Roberto’s coverage.   Regards, Margarita   From: Altamirano, Josh Sent: Thursday, December 18, 2025 3:08 PM To: Cervantes Dueas, Roberto Cc: Mayorga Macias, Walter A ; Olaya-cabrera, Margarita ; Desai, Chintan ; Aguirre Rodriguez, Sergio A ; Hernandez Miramontes, Jose Miguel ; Parks, Edwin E Subject: Re: Memory Size Variations for Boot and Reset   Hi Roberto,   These values are being reported by SVOS itself during boot. For example:   [    1.778747] smpboot: x86: Booting SMP configuration: [    1.779946] .... node  #0, CPUs:      #1 #2 #3 [    1.782497] smp: Brought up 1 node, 4 CPUs [    1.784188] smpboot: Total of 4 processors activated (19200.00 BogoMIPS) [    1.786433] Memory: 16452752K/16916016K available (13517K kernel code, 2439K rwdata, 9812K rodata, 3516K init, 4140K bss, 429616K reserved, 0K cma-reserved) [    1.790693] devtmpfs: initialized [    1.791777] ACPI: PM: Registering ACPI NVS region [mem 0x610cd000-0x61afcfff] (10682368 bytes)   Thanks, Josh From: Cervantes Dueas, Roberto Sent: Thursday, December 18, 2025 1:02 PM To: Altamirano, Josh Cc: Mayorga Macias, Walter A ; Olaya-cabrera, Margarita ; Desai, Chintan ; Aguirre Rodriguez, Sergio A ; Hernandez Miramontes, Jose Miguel ; Parks, Edwin E Subject: RE: Memory Size Variations for Boot and Reset   Hi Josh,   I suppose this is a behavior seen in pre-silicon, right? Which tools are you using to check those values? It would be interesting to get the dmesg output for the first boot and another for the subsequent boots. In the case of the GRUB option for pre-silicon, some memory regions are reserved (I don't remember the purpose of that), and I’m not sure if it could affect this behavior. But having the dmesg logs for both scenarios would be helpful in determining why this happens.   Saludos/Regards Roberto Cervantes   From: Altamirano, Josh Sent: Wednesday, December 17, 2025 12:12 PM To: Cervantes Dueas, Roberto Cc: Mayorga Macias, Walter A ; Olaya-cabrera, Margarita ; Desai, Chintan ; Aguirre Rodriguez, Sergio A ; Hernandez Miramontes, Jose Miguel ; Parks, Edwin E Subject: Re: Memory Size Variations for Boot and Reset   Hi Roberto,    We are noticing there is a memory difference in used/available from first boot to SVOS vs subsequent boots. However it seems to be consistent after the reboot     1st boot: Memory: 16395768K/16857992K available (14336K kernel code, 2442K rwdata, 9628K rodata, 3472K init, 4184K bss, 414964K reserved, 0K cma-reserved)   2nd boot (first reset):  Memory: 16382784K/16840864K available (14336K kernel code, 2442K rwdata, 9628K rodata, 3472K init, 4184K bss, 410820K reserved, 0K cma-reserved)   3rd boot (second reset):  Memory: 16382784K/16840864K available (14336K kernel code, 2442K rwdata, 9628K rodata, 3472K init, 4184K bss, 410820K reserved, 0K cma-reserved)   4th boot (third reset): Memory: 16382784K/16840864K available (14336K kernel code, 2442K rwdata, 9628K rodata, 3472K init, 4184K bss, 410820K reserved, 0K cma-reserved)       Couple different questions. First, why is the used/available different each time there is a first boot? Second, what is SVOS doing with the memory?   Thanks, Josh     From: Parks, Edwin E Sent: Wednesday, December 10, 2025 4:01 PM To: Hernandez Miramontes, Jose Miguel Cc: Mayorga Macias, Walter A ; Altamirano, Josh ; Olaya-cabrera, Margarita ; Desai, Chintan ; Aguirre Rodriguez, Sergio A Subject: Memory Size Variations for Boot and Reset   I filed this PreSighting based on my investigation into the uart.log data for 2nd cold boot: 22021820442 - [DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][SVOS] OS reporting different memory size/structure after reset   OS is reporting different memory sizes for every test I've executed on the same model.  I am also seeing that the memory size is always smaller after reset.  Is there any reason this might be happening?   Cold Reset Example: Boot:    16456528K/16916492K available   Reset:  16423912K/16880484K available   Delta:  -32616K/-35945K available   Warm Reset Example: Boot:    16456068K/16916020K available Reset:  16423976K/16880544K available   Delta:  -32092K/-35476K available   The uart.log files are attached to the preSighting for all of the runs that I looked at.  22611776494 acgarcia Reached out to   @Shah, Akshay S to see if there are any post-silicon logs that we can refer to.

raw.title: [DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][SVOS] BIOS reporting loss of CMOS data, so booting with defaults on 2nd cold boot test
raw.description: BIOS is reporting this information for the 2nd cold boot test: Start ConfigureGpio() for BootMode Detection. End ConfigureGpio() for BootMode Detection. RTC Power failure or CMOS check sum bad !! Could be due to a weak or missing battery. Setting BootMode to BOOT_WITH_DEFAULT_SETTINGS We want 2nd cold boot to boot in a different mode, so that it utilized all of the saved/restored data from 1st cold boot.  Using this PreSighting to track the solution to saving and restoring CMOS data for this TR. 2nd cold boot execution results: /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M5a/reset_trs_rev2/test_runs/14024874521_second_cold_boot.1

raw.comments: 22611620622 etsui Confirmed with the VTC/xtor team that the CAS tags are independent from MEM_WR. That would track with the behavior we've seen before wrt MEM_WR and MEM_RD behavior. Likely next step is to reach out to supercollider team to see what the error reported is complaining about.  22611644257 qata Just synced with Erikson on this.  Some latest findings.  1. We can reproduce this on MIO model - this will help speed up debug and waveform captures if needed (likely will need) 2. We still need to understand why it is okay for CAS/Atomics to target the same offsets within a cacheline - Is it because its atomics?  I think from here on, likely all debug experiments should be done on MIO for speed.  Next Step should be: - Get a waveform capture on MIO model of the CAS txn to see if RP is handling things correctly - Double check with PCIe team to see if CAS is actually supported by the RP - Confirm if it really is okay for CAS targeting same offsets within a cacheline is okay  22611677452 suratako @Ta, Quang A Please update the status on this pre-sighting  22611698437 qata filed sighting for supercollider team to help investigate the error code 6 issue that potentially looks more like a tools issue - https://hsdes.intel.com/appstore/article-one/#/article/22021981502 The error code from pcietclib looks generic without any description. post-si hit similar issue without atomics and the solution was to run with a later version of SC. 14025840127 - [DMR A0 PO][Coherency]PCIETC_ERR_NOT_FOUND Error on AN004022BMH2235 Lets try and rerun with an up to date version of supercollider to see if that resolves our issue.  22611774748 mzavalaa 22021981502 (parent-child) - link(s) are added via link tab.

Added the following to hsle_switch_rtl_cores.simics:     echo "GATING UCLK for DCM0 and DCM7 CBB0 trying to let RTL cores relinquish their UDI resource"     emu.reg_setvalue "soc_tb.soc.cbb0.cbb_compute_0.par_compute_core0_wrp.core.core_server_inst.par_pm.pm.mlbtrs.pmfgf_fil.mlfils.mlfilmains.pma_fil_crs.core_pma_fil_registers.registers.fil_control_1.uclk_gating_disable[0:0]" value = 0x0     emu.reg_setvalue "soc_tb.soc.cbb0.cbb_compute_3.par_compute_core7_wrp.core.core_server_inst.par_pm.pm.mlbtrs.pmfgf_fil.mlfils.mlfilmains.pma_fil_crs.core_pma_fil_registers.registers.fil_control_1.uclk_gating_disable[0:0]" value = 0x0 Now see that UDI returns correct status to avoid the timeout: [39m[3859135835]  CR_R  [    0x1540]           RESOURCE_OWN_REQ_STATUS_IP_CCF                                                                   0x0 <-- [0mUDI=0[0m [32m*VCCINF=0[0m [32m*SB_CLK=0[0m

model:  /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/joshalt/MCP/M5A/ipdisable/PCIe/test_runs/22020679425_ipdisable_bios_random_waveform.0/replay_capture.2 I opened Verdi in a VNC session for whoever needs to look at it.  22611741103 eeparks For CCF PMA to relinquish its UDI resource, it needs to enter Fast C3 and hold it. One of the conditions for entering Fast C3 is that all cores are not active. CCF PMA uses the core clock request as the indication for that. In your test, 4 cores are holding their clock request asserted, and therefore, CCF PMA can’t enter Fast C3. As a result, it doesn’t relinquish the UDI resource.  22611741105 eeparks Thanks Shlomi for the debug,   Next question: how can we check why the cores are not de-asserting the clock request? eeparks Ray suspects that there are some forces used in HSLE that might be causing the core clocks to stay on, so he is creating a run that will manage the forces around reset.  22611760174 eeparks Confirmed that clock gating forced as part of HSLE boot was causing this issue. Added the following to hsle_switch_rtl_cores.simics:     echo "GATING UCLK for DCM0 and DCM7 CBB0 trying to let RTL cores relinquish their UDI resource"     emu.reg_setvalue "soc_tb.soc.cbb0.cbb_compute_0.par_compute_core0_wrp.core.core_server_inst.par_pm.pm.mlbtrs.pmfgf_fil.mlfils.mlfilmains.pma_fil_crs.core_pma_fil_registers.registers.fil_control_1.uclk_gating_disable[0:0]" value = 0x0     emu.reg_setvalue "soc_tb.soc.cbb0.cbb_compute_3.par_compute_core7_wrp.core.core_server_inst.par_pm.pm.mlbtrs.pmfgf_fil.mlfils.mlfilmains.pma_fil_crs.core_pma_fil_registers.registers.fil_control_1.uclk_gating_disable[0:0]" value = 0x0 Now see that UDI returns correct status to avoid the timeout: [39m[3859135835]  CR_R  [    0x1540]           RESOURCE_OWN_REQ_STATUS_IP_CCF                                                                   0x0 <-- [0mUDI=0[0m [32m*VCCINF=0[0m [32m*SB_CLK=0[0m

articles.subject: hsdes_sighting

raw.title: [DMR][HIOP][HIOP][MCP HSLE] x4x4x8 Bifurcation Enabling on MCP Multistack M5A model
raw.description: Bhawana is trying to enable x4x4x8 Bifurcation on the Multistack M5A model, and we've hit a few things that we're going to start tracking via a presighting to document the things we ran into and the solutions we needed to do to resolve them. List of things we hit so far and how we resolved them: 1. PCIe Cards not training up -> fix the bios knobs for Port IsEnabled  2. All but 2 cards not training up -> Fix the link train simics script to trigger on a later string to allow bios to actually hit the start training button on all the ports 3. BIOS needs to reboot due to IO resource rebalancing -> Current issue
raw.subject: sighting
raw.comments: 22611698018 qata Trying to reproduce issue #3 in pure VP, but need to get the configuration correct.  VP was currently only showing pcie_slot_3a without all the extra ports within that slot.  We got some BKM's from Smitha Vanga, and Alexander Kaindin: You don’t need to use compatibility params file. The best way is to follow this example when you create your script: docs.intel.com/documents/vp_simulation/capabilities/simics/platform/dmr/dmr-rio-7/target_guide_oakstream.html   # my_script.simics: $bios = other_bios_1.bin $bios2 = other_bios_2.bin $yml_script = "oakstream-rio/main" (load-target $yml_script     bios=$bios     bios2=$bios2 )     Best regards, Alex --------------- The file targets/oakstream/oakstream.target.yml already exists in your pkg folder where you installed the simics .  Don’t add that file in your custom scripts. Also, simics has moved away from .simics files. We suggest using the .yml presets for the configuration.   The other way is if not modifying the .yml then edit or check file targets/oakstream/compatibility_params from custom-rio.simics   Check if the below params are present in the targets/oakstream/compatibility_params.include"   file, if not present add below to the compatibility_params.include.         ["pcie_configuration", "pcie_configuration"],         ["pcie_gen4_configuration", "pcie_gen4_configuration"],         Next add the below to your custom-rio.simics file   For example :   $pcie_configuration = "0a,0b,0c,0d,0e,0f,0g,0h,1a,1b,1c,1d,1e,1f,1g,1h,2a,2b,2c,2d,2e,2f,2g,2h,3a,3b,3c,3d,3e,3f,3g,3h:0a,0b,0c,0d,0e,0f,0g,0h,1a,1b,1c,1d,1e,1f,1g,1h,2a,2b,2c,2d,2e,2f,2g,2h,3a,3b,3c,3d,3e,3f,3g,3h "   Thanks, Smitha Eventually Bhawana, got all of the ports showing in pure VP, now we just need to connect the cards up in the intended configuration and reproduce the IO rebalancing issue.  """ I have copied three files from central repo to local oakstream-rio.simics, main.target.yml, compatibilty_params.include and made the changes as smitha told, it worked well. in oakstream I only change the run script pathing and add the pcie configuration as required, in main.target.yml change the pcie configs and in compatibilty param include         ["pcie_configuration", "pcie_configuration"],        ["pcie_gen4_configuration", "pcie_gen4_configuration"], in $compatible_params  """  22611700330 qata Issue is reproduced in pure VP simics using the original configuration from HSLE MCP: [IIO](PCI) Start trying high values for IOSize - 0x4-8 and 0x0 for Auto 2. Might start getting in touch with someone from BIOS team to help us understand if there are any ways to avoid BIOS having to request a rebalance reboot.  22611702647 qata When Bhawana tried the rest of the values, it seems like the results were all the same and that we were always getting another reboot and rebalance required and reboot required.  Bhawana will attach the uart logs for each of the settings to the HSD and we'll need to get someone from BIOS involved to help us figure out a solution to this.  22611706694 qata Looks like none of the values for the current config will work based off of feedback from Wilmaison: """ The emulation configuration you are running is not valid or the endpoint devices are not set to the correct configuration. Your config is asking for 100KB of legacy IO and the whole system only supports 64KB.   So changing the knob won’t do anything to satisfy device requirements. Normally we would skip the reboot and continue to boot without satisfying the attached device requirements to allow BIOS setup menu changes. Not sure why that is missing from the log you have captured if that feature is still active.     Thanks, Wilmaison """ I don't know how he got to 100KB of legacy IO space needed, but it sounds like its currently not possible with the config of 24 cards total that we have for this.  I'd like to figure out how it got to the 100KB mark for this, but for now we're going to reduce the amount of LCD's in the system while still keeping capability for P2P and the bifurcation needed: IMH0 - 2 stacks of x4x4x8     stack 1 - (0 in simics VP)     stack 4 - (3 in simics VP) IMH1 - 2 stacks of x4x4x8     stack 1 - (0 in simics VP)     stack 4 - (3 in simics VP) This should basically cut the number of cards in half, and if 24 cards were amounting to 100KB, this should cut that in half also and we should hopefully be below the 64KB that Wilmaison was mentioning. If this works, Bhawana will also attempt kicking off an MCP HSLE run with the updated config.  22611722756 qata We resolved this as a configuration issue.  BKM is the following: 1. Disable IoSize for any port that is not actively in use - using the IoSize bios knob 2. Set the IoSize correctly (4k per device per stack) - up to a total of 64K for the entire system 3. Update the bifurcation.dut_cfg file so that it matches the BIOS knob for bifurcation settings in terms of the order of ports 4. Update Port Enables (IsEnabled) bios knobs for the ports that are in use and disable the ports that are NOT in use With this - we're able to get the system booted in the correct bifurcation now into SVOS.  Bhawana: """ When I added the 8th port io size disable it explicitly than I think it worked well and now it is not showing 1000 IoSize for 0.08 Even I added stack0 of imh1 and after that too it worked well and not hit the rebalance issue  """  22611722768 qata /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_007/users/bhawana/imh_2/mcp_ici_hsle_svos_vtc_fmods.3  - Bhawana's been able to get a good run going with MCP multistack in the configuration and bifurcation that we need.   - This run was purely to double check that we're able to boot in the configuration that we need.  Next we're going to try and start running the content that we need - /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/bhawana/qos/hiop_qos_msi_p2p.py  - We need to update the content with the new bus numbers and target maps.  pythonsv_enable pythonsv.logging pythonsv.base simics pythonsv.start_cycle 6000000000 pythonsv.path /nfs/site/disks/ive_pysv_dmr_001/pythonsv_static_repos/ap2_mcp_m5a_py310/ pythonsv.script /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/bhawana/qos/hiop_qos_msi_p2p.py pythonsv.stub /nfs/site/disks/xpg_dmrmcp_0152/users/adgronqu/DMR/PythonSV/INI/AP2_M5A/ICI/pysv_config.ini This section needs to be updated with the new bus# and target maps: 322             # VTC Configuration Parameters 323             bus0 = 0x21 324             bus4 = 0x121 325             buses = [bus0, bus4] 326             bus_to_targmap = {bus0 : 0x1542_4000_0000, 327                               bus4 : 0x1120_C000_0000 328                               }  1566814949 qata I think we can now close this HSD with the bifurcation configuration now enabled with the BKMs listed prior comments.

raw.description: Tenant: sighting_central.sighting, Forum = pss.model .  Not in Scope = IMH SLE, MCP DFX SLE (Tenant = server.support). CBB SLE contact Pavel Mikhlin Description Field Section: Problem Statement: Expected: LCD VM0 continues to complete the rest of the programmed Vectors until it finishes and then sets the VM End Status to 1 Observed: LCD VM0 for some Ardens are suddenly stopping execution of Vectors and never getting to the point of setting the VM End Status to 1 Path to Logfile: Rundir: /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/mcp_multistack/manual/cross_imh/22020670195_hiop_p2p_cross_imh_base.3 Failure signature: ^[[?2004l^MERROR: stopPciLibAgent(/sv/arden-01/vm0): pcieTC_vm_checkDonePoll: PCIETC_TIMEOUT^M ERROR: stopPciLibAgent(/sv/arden-05/vm0): pcieTC_vm_checkDonePoll: PCIETC_TIMEOUT^M ERROR: stopPciLibAgent(/sv/arden-07/vm0): pcieTC_vm_checkDonePoll: PCIETC_TIMEOUT^M And final Upstream MEM Transaction is NOT the expected final vector: For arden-01 - for)=0.000020 seconds  22611682241 qata Seems like all of the VM's did actually start and were attempted to be stopped by Supercollider. Because we can see the MEM_WR's to start the VM's and then ~1.5M cycles later we see the writes to stop the VM's.  What is noticeable is that the threads that have the TIMEOUT are the ones where Supercollider had to do a bit of polling yet at the end it still sees the VM being active.  I'm wondering if it's just taking some extra time to drain out and get the replies for the P2P accesses.  Will do another test, where we run a script right after Supercollider finishes and just continues to poll the VM statuses to see if it ever finishes. Will go ahead and also write the enable bit back to the VM's because Supercollider sets it to disable at the end.  -------- I also don't see any arguments to Supercollider where I can modify the amount of time it is polling the VTC VM statuses before it determines its TIMEOUT. Its just a library call from the ArdenAPI.  22611682242 qata In the case of the DDIO test - /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/mcp_multistack/manual/ddio/22020670198_hiop_ddio_lock_fetchadd.0  - It seems like it might be a different issue altogether - might be a VTC problem because it doesn't seem like this is correct where the TAG is being reused between the CAS and the MEM_WR  8521375312 U         CAS --- ---- 252 5900 0000 ---- 0 ---- 00000005 00500030 ---- - 0 0 0 --------------- 0 0     8 6E000008 59000000 00000005 00500030 0 -------- -------- - 00 00 --- -- ---- ----  --------- - ----------- --- ---- --- ---- ---- ---- - ---- -------- -------- ---- - - - - --------------- - -     0 00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000 - -------- -------- - -- -- --- -- ---- ----  --------- - ----------- --- ---- --- ---- ---- ---- - ---- -------- -------- ---- - - - - --------------- - -     4 02000000 00000000 00000000 00000000 - -------- -------- - -- -- --- -- ---- ----  --------- - ----------- --- ---- --- ---- ---- ---- - ---- -------- -------- ---- - - - -              H0 - - ----- 6E000008 59000000 00000005 00500030 - -------- -------- - -- -- --- -- ---- ----  8521375472 U      MEM_WR --- ---- 253 5900 0000 ---- 0 ---- 00000005 00500070 ---- - 0 0 0 --------------- 0 0     4 60000004 59000000 00000005 00500070 0 -------- -------- - 00 00 --- -- ---- ----  --------- - ----------- --- ---- --- ---- ---- ---- - ---- -------- -------- ---- - - - - --------------- - -     0 38003940 3A003B40 3C003D40 3E003F40 - -------- -------- - -- -- --- -- ---- ----  --------- - ----------- --- ---- --- ---- ---- ---- - ---- -------- -------- ---- - - - -              H0 - - ----- 60000004 59000000 00000005 00500070 - -------- -------- - -- -- --- -- ---- ---- CAS is considered an Atomic (part of the test for DDIO here) and all Atomics are supposed to be non-posted, which all require completions before reusing the TAG. Will probably try and reproduce this in simics, but likely this one might just go to the VTC team to take a look at.  We'll continue to debug the TIMEOUT issue when that run finishes.  22611682240 suratako @Ta, Quang A  Please update the next steps on this PS. Has this been escalated to a different val tenant, no links other than TR's?  22611682243 qata After writing the script that parses through the pcie logs for analysis, it doesn't seem like there's anything pending by the end of the test. So there shouldn't really be any reason why the VMMR's would still indicate that the VM's are running. There's something maybe internally... or when Supercollider tries to clear the VM enable bit after it fails the timeout that messes up the VM statuses at the end of the test. Since the pythonsv polling scripts didn't work and it looked like the statuses all said that the VM's were still running at the end of the test.  I think for now... we have to treat this as a special case because we can't change supercollider code itself to not try to disable the VM's if it times out. We'll have an identifier in the tool to and will probably add a signature to kokua to identify this with no real possible solutions at this time. We don't have a good way to extend Supercollider's polling time right now and we'd need to file an enhancement ticket either to Supercollider team or the pcietc library team to allow Supercollider to give us some option to increase the poll time in these cases or some option where Supercollider will not try to disable the VM's if it timeout so that we can check the final status of the VM's at the end of the run without something possibly bad being done in the middle.  So really... I think the enhancement request we need to make to Supercollider team is: 1. Provide some option where - if supercollider see's a pcietc timeout when polling for the vm status -> it does not go and try to set the vmx_enable=0 or any other bit in the vm_mode register and instead just continue on with the rest of supercollider cleanup.      - This is to allow users to be able to go back in later in the case of slow progress p2p commands to check if eventually the VM status goes to stop during infinite mode.  22611682246 qata Actually... I think the status of the VM is contingent on the fact that all of the vectors finish for a given loop. This is seen in the single passing case for PRd in the run -  /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/mcp_multistack/manual/cross_imh/22020670195_hiop_p2p_cross_imh_base.3 Only arden-01, 05, 07 hit the PCIETC Timeout. While arden-03 (which is the only PRd that didn't hit the PCIETC TIMEOUT) finishes. When you compare a passing PRd thread and one of the failing PRd threads it's actually quite obvious something happened on the VM side to where it just stopped sending out MEM_RD's even though CMPLT_D's are returning later. So then it will never set the VM end status to be done. I think maybe actually we should bring this up with VTC team as a potential issue that maybe just warrants more investigation on the VTC side asking why doesn't the failing VM's finish its vector loop in order to set the end status eventually.  ^[[u^[[0J^[[44;1mSUPERCOLLIDER:^[[0m Ways=4   Sets=64     DfltAddrAlgo=DFT                   Time=000:00:30^M ^M Thrd    Type    Ways  Sets Tgt Of Attr    Instr     Agent                     Loops^M ---- ---------- ---- ----- --- -- ---- ----------- ------------------------ ----------^M    0       Idle    4    64   0  0   WB     default S-1 C-1 T-1   pid=   2404     2671459^M    1        PWr    4    63   5 28      /sv/arden-00/vm0^M    2        PRd    4    63   6 23      /sv/arden-01/vm0^M    3        PWr    4    63   7 26      /sv/arden-02/vm0^M    4        PRd    4    63   8 15      /sv/arden-03/vm0^M    5        PWr    4    63   1 41      /sv/arden-04/vm0^M    6        PRd    4    63   2 37      /sv/arden-05/vm0^M    7        PWr    4    63   3 54      /sv/arden-06/vm0^M    8        PRd    4    63   4 30      /sv/arden-07/vm0^M ^M Note: Updater=t0. raw.title: [DMR][HIOP][HIOP][MCP HSLE] PCIe RP Logging a Fatal Error for EP=1 from Device
raw.description: /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/mcp_multistack/m5c/14024453913_p2p_poison_nopcieinj.0 In this test, all we're basically doing is P2P traffic where each VM is doing P2P with first MEM_WRITE and then MEM_READ.  We randomly pick which VM's are going to do EP=1 in the MEM_WRITE vector.  The PCIe RP's are logging fatal errors upon receiving the EP=1 MEM_WRITE commands. While I think its expected that the PCIe RP should be logging something, I don't think it should be logging fatal for a data poison case.  12-30 10:44:RESULT   :=== EP=1 Injection Summary === 12-30 raw.subject: sighting
raw.comments: 22611696481 eeparks 22020679428 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611696483 eeparks 22020679425 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611696499 eeparks 22020679303 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  1667227717 arvindnr This error is also seen in null engine -  22611716542 alee8 This was addressed via email to  @Rao, Arvind  The title is incorrect: BMC does not report "unsupported request" , that's S3M doing that. BMC BMOD (not FMOD) will report no memory mapped within BMC boundaries on that range, most likely, the MMBI range which is again, a known limitation in BMC BMOD. If a successful access is needed, do use BMC FMOD following or using the advertised targets from the DMR RIO release notes.  1667281927 arvindnr Cloned to simics_platform tenant where a valid passing configuration was identified on Simics VP. Requires 3 fmods - s3m, oobmsm, bmc Additional BIOS knobs to be enabled. Need to bring in a fix based on the above for HSLE where currently only s3m fmod is enabled. oobmsm and bmc are bmods. Limitations: OOBMSM fmod doesn't support null engine / HSLE. BMC fmod would increase boot time.  22611769132 jsingh3 Simics ticket has been rejected on 2/6, what are the next steps/.  1667306952 arvindnr @Singh, Jaswinder As we have updated in the ticket, issue has been root caused and validated as the configuration issue where we need certain BIOS knobs and the fmods to go over the unsupported request from S3M error. Hence this ticket should be closed. Please help us in closing this ticket. As a full solution for future projects we have requested the configuration to be made available in bmod for BMC. For that we have raised an HSD which will be a low priority for now and it will be implemented based on the future bandwidth. Pasting the HSD link for the closure of this HSD -  16029871440 - [DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][SVOS] Require bmod enhancement to support MMBI accesses

raw.comments: 22611696480 eeparks 22020679428 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611696484 eeparks 22020679425 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611696498 eeparks 22020679303 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  1667228380 knshah Observations: The issue is observed in MCP models for both AP1 and AP2. It is to do with the core configurations.  In the case of MCP models(AP1 & AP2), we enable 16 cores. These 16 cores are assigned in this manner First core from CBB0, Second core from CBB1, Third core from CBB2, Fourth core from CBB3, Fifth core from CBB0 and so and so forth. Hence, other cores from CBB1, CBB2 and CBB3 needs to be properly disabled via fuse. Now, with the provided run area, tried running null mode to reproduce the issue and was able to reproduce it.  logs: /nfs/site/disks/pse_oks_002/knshah/DMR/A0/HSD_Debug/22021977764/null_run/RTC_ESE_29D90_Svos_ColdReset_SEQ_0_4T_C4M2_fmods_vtc.0 As an experiment, tried enabling just 2 cores from CBB0 and could see that it is no longer showing those spec-viol. logs: /nfs/site/disks/pse_oks_002/knshah/DMR/A0/HSD_Debug/22021977764/null_run/RTC_ESE_29D90_Svos_ColdReset_SEQ_0_4T_C4M2_fmods_vtc.2  1667228531 knshah Similar HSD (for a different project) 16017119770 - [LNL][Simics] CPU: MSR 0x151 access in unpowered processor returns fault Snippet of relevant section:  22611704993 eeparks 16017119770 (related-link) - link(s) are added via link tab.  1667247558 knshah Based on further debug, it is observed that even the issue continues to exist in VP, Null and HSLE environment. Please note that earlier experiments with 2 cores and 4 cores have the issues. But, those are moved at a later stage. (hence, ignore my earlier comment that it has worked with 2 core setup) As this issue is seen in Simics, we will include simics team for further debug.  22611736193 jsingh3 2/2 - it has not been confirmed by simics team hence status changed to open  22611741627 eeparks Further debugging shows that the message comes because the BIOS issues a WRMSR instruction to these cores for MSR 0x151 (MSR_BIOS_DONE) Therefore, the behavior for unpowered/disabled cores in Simics is to showcase that message telling that a WRMSR instruction was sent to a powered off core.   There is no Simics model bug here... it's an information message, and it's expected to appear if you are disabling any number of cores. If using Core disable by fuse, is it expected behavior by Software/BIOS to send a WRMSR to MSR_BIOS_DONE to ALL cores, independent of their enable/disabled state. Or should SW/BIOS be able to detect which cores are disabled and discern to which ones send the WRMSR instruction.  1566840735 amadaan Updates are being tracked in Simics Tenant HSD: 16029741260 - [DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][SVOS] MSR 0x151 access in unpowered processor returns fault Will update here once it's resolved.

raw.comments: 22611696478 eeparks 22020679428 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611696485 eeparks 22020679425 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611696497 eeparks 22020679303 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  14615100987 rrnavacu Adding updates from related e-mail thread: This might be your reference to use: https://wiki.ith.intel.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=1638645317#UARTService-LegacyCOMPorts    In particular, table 4:     Last two conditions describe the cases where FW will terminate the UART IO access with unsupported request (UR) which is what you are seeing.     - Alejandro   From: Lee, Alejandro Sent: Tuesday, February 10, 2026 1:27 PM To: Rao, Arvind ; s3m-vp Cc: Narayan, Muralidhar ; Nava Cuellar, Raymundo R ; Shah, Kartik N ; Parks, Edwin E Subject: Re: [DMR] UR detected in S3M during access to IO address   These logs don't represent a functional issue.   The logs are also not new: we've seen them since a while. The suspicion is that there's some legacy UART driver in Linux attempting to access this register when UART IO is not enabled OR the S3M FW does truly not support the register.   If you want to go in depth in fixing this issue, I'd suggest start identifying whether S3M FW supports this register 0xF within UART IO; then check with SVOS team or even Centos to find potential consumers of this register.   May I ask what is your end goal?   - Alejandro     From: Rao, Arvind Sent: Tuesday, February 10, 2026 8:01 AM To: s3m-vp Cc: Narayan, Muralidhar ; Nava Cuellar, Raymundo R ; Shah, Kartik N ; Parks, Edwin E Subject: [DMR] UR detected in S3M during access to IO address   Hi,   We observe the below errors with the S3M fmod while booting to SVOS with the 1_socket_ucc-generic target for DMR RIO. It appears that the IOSF PRI accesses to these IO addresses are returning completion status 1.   Please advise if there is any additional Simics setting that needs to be applied here or if this is a known issue.   [oakstream_rio.mb.s3m0.s3m[0].xreg.main_p warning] {oakstream_rio.mb.s3m0.s3m[0].arc_uc 0x100c0e7a 23808054441073} [22:59:22.1766] UR detected for IO address 0x00000000000003EF! [oakstream_rio.mb.s3m0.s3m[0].xreg.main_p warning] {oakstream_rio.mb.s3m0.s3m[0].arc_uc 0x100c0e7a 23808054480894} [22:59:22.4053] UR detected for IO address 0x00000000000003EF! [oakstream_rio.mb.s3m0.s3m[0].xreg.main_p warning] {oakstream_rio.mb.s3m0.s3m[0].arc_uc 0x100c0e7a 23808054521074} [22:59:22.4066] UR detected for IO address 0x00000000000002EF! [oakstream_rio.mb.s3m0.s3m[0].xreg.main_p warning] {oakstream_rio.mb.s3m0.s3m[0].arc_uc 0x100c0e7a 23808054560892} [22:59:22.6204] UR detected for IO address 0x00000000000002EF!   Regards, Arvind  1667305812 arvindnr eSPI enable/disable was checked in the softstraps and was found to be enabled. (Bit 29 of 0x10082881 is 0 => eSPI enabled) @Lee, Alejandro Could you please confirm if this is sufficient or we need to check anything else wrt eSPI?  22611779708 alee8 ESPI is needed to be enabled.  1667335523 skrishn1 https://wiki.ith.intel.com/spaces/PCHLESS/pages/1638645317/UART+Service#UARTService-Table2-LegacyCOMI%2FOPorts To Look at eSPI registers IOD: Offset 0x80 Value 0x10 IOE: Offset 0x82 Value 0x3f0f https://wiki.ith.intel.com/spaces/PCHLESS/pages/1375899788/eSPI+Service#eSPIService-I%2FOEnables(IOE)-Offset82h https://wiki.ith.intel.com/spaces/PCHLESS/pages/1375899788/eSPI+Service#eSPIService-I%2FODecodeRanges(IOD)-Offset80h After changing the value of IOD: From the above experiment, eSPI chip currently supports only 2 COM ports that is COM A and COM B, And due to this reason, it can be only connected to any two of the ports from COM 1, 2, 3, 4, Of which their respective ranges can be used. And when there is a IO access to the other two unmapped range there will be a UR issued by the S3M.

raw.comments: 22611696479 eeparks 22020679428 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611696487 eeparks 22020679425 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611696496 eeparks 22020679303 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  1667228464 knshah Observations: Tried running the setup in NULL mode. This issue is observed in null run as well. On further investigation, found to be a similar issue reported to simics team.  HSD: 16026465047 - [DMR]{CentOS][Perf] List of unknown MSR that Signaling GP fault Observation/Conclusion from the HSD about this MSR:  22611704979 eeparks 16026465047 (related-link) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611704987 eeparks 14024592188 (related-link) - link(s) are added via link tab.  1667233793 knshah I tried with 6.18 Kernel version (latest SVOS image from 21-Jan-2026). Unfortunately, the issue still exists. logs for reference: /nfs/site/disks/pse_oks_002/knshah/DMR/A0/HSD_Debug/22021977764/null_run/RTC_ESE_29D90_Svos_ColdReset_SEQ_0_4T_C4M2_fmods_vtc.9 I will reach out to SVOS kernel support person to get details about the same.  22611721246 acpreble So are these MSRs MSR_ATOM_CORE_RATIOS and MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG? They look to be very fundamental. I think the definition for it goes back to 2017 in arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h. Is there some reason those are not implemented in Simics? That message is Simics saying it's going to cause a #GP because it's not recognizing those MSRs. So the kernel just winds up having to react to the fault. Are you trying to get a special kernel that has lobotomized out code that's trying to use that stuff? You'd presumably have to strip out all the power management code. I wouldn't know what kernel build configs could do that (if they even can). That would have to be an iterative process and I'd eventually probably have to just hand off a strategy for that to you to refine if you really need a kernel that doesn't touch those MSRs.  1667241299 knshah Hi @Preble, Adam C, Thanks for your inputs. In this HSD, we were looking more about 0xc0011029 MSR which is resulting in GP Fault.  We have 2 questions (if you can help us to get the answers): 1. Why AMD-specific MSR is being accessed by kernel? 2. Is there a way to blacklist/skip this specific MSR getting accessed at all? Some more details about 0xc0011029 MSR:  22611723921 acpreble A filtered grep in 6.18 for that MSR: arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG               0xc0011029 arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT   1 arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE      BIT_ULL(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT) arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_ZEN2_FP_BACKUP_FIX_BIT 9 arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c:      msr_set_bit(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, 31); arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c:              msr_set_bit(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_ZEN2_FP_BACKUP_FIX_BIT); arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c:              msr_clear_bit(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_ZEN2_FP_BACKUP_FIX_BIT); arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c:              msr_set_bit(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c:                          MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT); arch/x86/kernel/cpu/hygon.c:            msr_set_bit(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, arch/x86/kernel/cpu/hygon.c:                        MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT); arch/x86/kvm/svm/svm.c: case MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG: arch/x86/kvm/svm/svm.c:                 *data |= MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE; arch/x86/kvm/svm/svm.c: case MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG: arch/x86/kvm/svm/svm.c: case MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG: { arch/x86/kvm/x86.c:     MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, arch/x86/power/cpu.c:           { MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG,      X86_FEATURE_LFENCE_RDTSC }, tools/arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG         0xc0011029 tools/arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT     1 tools/arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE        BIT_ULL(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT) tools/arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_ZEN2_FP_BACKUP_FIX_BIT 9 tools/testing/selftests/kvm/x86/feature_msrs_test.c:    return msr != MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG; The places where I see it straight up touching that MSR are all in arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c. I would think if it's getting that far, the kernel decided you're running an AMD processor. I'm guessing you're not hehehe. My first bit would be to skim dmesg and see in the startup if it's declaring the CPU (it think) it's using. If it's screwing that up then everybody gets to figure out if Simics isn't reporting it properly and/or the kernel's not detecting it properly.  1667242350 knshah Strange to see that the artifactory for SVOS image contains the name "amd64" in its naming convention! Path: Index of simics-repos/ts/images/dmr/svos/202601261346 I can see that we do not have any connection with AMD64 w.r.t. cores in the system.  14615002846 acpreble Debian is using AMD64 to representing the 64-bit x86-based architecture. They've been doing that since it was first introduced as a package target back in 2007. It has nothing to do with targeting specifically AMD processors.

articles.list_title: [DMR][HIOP][HIOP][MCP HSLE] CPU Ramless Targeting in HSLE causes massive efficiency dip in emulator cycles
articles.subject: hsdes_sighting

raw.title: [DMR][HIOP][HIOP][MCP HSLE] CPU Ramless Targeting in HSLE causes massive efficiency dip in emulator cycles
raw.description: Motivation:  - HIOP has test cases where we need to have CPU threads issuing Memory Rd/Wr transactions out to both the PCIe VTC ramless space as well as to normal DRAM memory space. This is to exercise snoop conflict flows as well as credit/vc congestion.  Problem:   - When we try any supercollider run where we have a CPU thread issuing Memory Traffic either to DRAM space (mapped to RTL to actually exercise logic) or to VTC MMIO space, we see a significant drop in emulator efficiency to execute cycles where it goes down to ~10% testbench efficiency. Causing MOPs to take longer and longer Expectation:  - Testbench efficiency typically hovers around 20% to 50% when we run with just PCIe initiating traffic upstream to memory space/p2p.  - Also expecting that the MOPs being sent by the cores return within reasonable amount of time in relation to how many cycles it takes to return.    - Meaning - if previous MOPs are taking less than a minute to complete but are taking 6M cycles, a MOP later that is taking 200K cycles should also be taking less than a minute to complete. Example of rundir where ramless targeting is used and shows testbench efficiency at 10%: /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/mcp_multistack/m5c/22020670193_p2p_ramless_funnel.6  =================================  Job Summary  ===================================  Job id         : 72895918                   Class: ((SLES12)) && (((ZSE5 && 28B))) Qslot: /prj/sv/dmr/sysval/interactive Executed on    : fmez5132                   Pool : fm_zse5_g506              Starting time  : Mon Jan 12 09:40:29 2026          Qwait:  24h:47m:17s       Finishing time : Tue Jan 13 06:47:16 2026                                    Exit Status    : 0                                                             Total Job Elapsed Time: 21h 6m 47s TESTBENCH EFFICIENCY: 10 % EFFICIENCY SCORE: 1.1912 In this Run directory - we can see from the testbench, big differences in MOP completions in relation to real time and cycles:  - Early on in the run we can see there are MMCFG/MMIO MOPs taking less than a minute to complete with a cycle difference of Millions of cycles  - Later on when ramless cpu threads start from supercollider, it takes 10's of minutes to complete a MOP that only needs thousands of cycles (vs. millions prior) mop_analysis.log: [idi_hybrid_comp_s0_cbb0_Core0] {oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[0][0]} [12:01:49.0118]  Waiting for  mop addr = 0x1010ffd11260 , size = 0x4 , op_type = read  cycle : (start 5376481782 , current 5381730000 ) , tag 205 | Difference: 5248218 | Tag usage: 0 mins 12.561 secs [idi_hybrid_comp_s0_cbb0_Core0] {oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[0][0]} [12:59:39.5318]  Waiting for  mop addr = 0xfec00010 , size = 0x4 , op_type = read  cycle : (start 7490766342 , current 7497614372 ) , tag 39 | Difference: 6848030 | Tag usage: 0 mins 18.823 secs ... [idi_hybrid_comp_s0_cbb0_Core0] {oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[0][0]} [03:29:49.0086]  Waiting for  mop addr = 0x154240000330 , size = 0x8 , op_type = read  cycle : (start 8325600105 , current 8325916772 ) , tag 66 | Difference: 316667 | Tag usage: 18 mins 12.001 secs [idi_hybrid_comp_s0_cbb0_Core0] {oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[0][0]} [05:05:10.0128]  Waiting for  mop addr = 0x154240000378 , size = 0x8 , op_type = read  cycle : (start 8326867709 , current 8327183245 ) , tag 70 | Difference: 315536 | Tag usage: 25 mins 12.006 secs Example of rundir where there are no cpu threads running in the supercollider command and testbench efficiency is much higher at 61%: /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/mcp_multistack/manual/cross_imh/22020670195_hiop_p2p_cross_imh_base.4  =================================  Job Summary  ===================================  Job id         : 72793286                   Class: ((SLES12)) && (((ZSE5 && 25B))) Qslot: /prj/sv/dmr/sysval/interactive Executed on    : fmez5183                   Pool : fm_zse5_g509              Starting time  : Sun Jan 11 09:14:46 2026          Qwait:  47h:40m:45s       Finishing time : Sun Jan 11 13:30:06 2026                                    Exit Status    : 0                                                             Total Job Elapsed Time: 4h 15m 20s TESTBENCH EFFICIENCY: 61 % EFFICIENCY SCORE: 5.5823 In this rundir - we can see that there are still MOPs that supercollider has to send to the PCIe to check status on the VTC's, but they do not take nearly as long as the previous run directory:  - Even the longest MOP is only 2 minutes 30 seconds real time to finish with 600K cycles mop_analysis.log: [idi_hybrid_comp_s0_cbb0_Core0] {oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[0][0]} [12:59:06.6968]  Waiting for  mop addr = 0x1120c50e5000 , size = 0x4 , op_type = read  cycle : (start 7418430480 , current 7419038343 ) , tag 148 | Difference: 607863 | Tag usage: 2 mins 29.923 secs

raw.comments: 22611717824 eeparks 22020679425 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  1667247601 knshah Details about the MCA: Field Name Field Access Field Width Field Offset Field Default Current Value Field Description MCACOD RW/V/P 16 0 0x0 0x0412 MCA Error Code: IOSF error 0x0E0B general error 0x0407 Sunpass error - 0x0406 MSCOD RW/V/P 16 16 0x0 0x001A Model Specific Error Code - Encoded field for errors OI RW/V/P 5 32 0x0 0x0 Other Info - Always 0 FW_UPD RW/V/P 1 37 0x0 0x0 if HW error can overwrite the contents of the MCA Bank based on overwrite rules, this bit is cleared. Ucode can also write due to attribute into this register. CEC RW/V/P 15 38 0x0 0x0 Corrected Error Count CESI RW/V/P 2 53 0x0 0x0 Threshold-based error status AR RW/V/P 1 55 0x0 0x0 Recovery Action Required S RW/V/P 1 56 0x0 0x0 Signals the reporting of uncorrected, but recoverable (UCR) error in the MC bank. PCC RW/V/P 1 57 0x0 0x1 Processor Context Corrupt ADDRV RW/V/P 1 58 0x0 0x1 MCi_Addr_Valid - Always 0 MISCV RW/V/P 1 59 0x0 0x1 MCi_MISC Valid - Will be set for IOMCA error EN RW/V/P 1 60 0x0 0x0 Error Reporting Enabled - Set if corresponding MCi_CTL bit was set UC RW/V/P 1 61 0x0 0x1 Uncorrected error OVER RW/V/P 1 62 0x0 0x1 Error Overflow - Set to 1 if an error occurs where a valid error is already logged VAL RW/V/P 1 63 0x0 0x1 MCi_Status Valid - Set to 1 when valid error occurs  14615063571 rrnavacu Adding Alberto from RAS validation team According to the latest DMR Errort Tracker Rev1 1p0 file, this is the decoding of MCACOD 0x412 Kartik identified that UBR is the source of the error propagation to RASIP using GBSP tracker but MSCOD 0x001A is not listed in DMR Errort Tracker Rev1 1p0 file.  14615083433 amcortes From: https://docs.intel.com/documents/iparch/scf/HAS/Gen4/HAS/BRIDGE/Latest/HAS/SCF_GEN4_LATEST_BRIDGE_HAS.html MCACOD Specifies the machine-check architecture-defined error code for the machine-check error condition detected. The machine-check architecture defined error codes are guaranteed to be the same for all IA-32 processors that implement the machine-check architecture.For Bridge this field is always marked as 0x412 in the Full Bank MCA message Error Codes MCACOD must be 0x412 for Bridge If we confirm: ubr_error_log.MSCOD26 == 0x1 Then: invalid_meshid This error is flagged for the below 2 cases. The ‘invalid’ ID is based on the NIP_*SUPPORTED_MESHID parameter, ie; NIP_SUPPORTED_MESH_ID for Non-P2P and NIP_P2P_SUPPORTED_MESH_ID for P2P transactions. Received a transaction from NIP with an invalid Source MeshID. The packet is dropped and credits are not returned as the Source ID is invalid. For BL messages, the 2nd pump could trigger another error as it will be considered as a new message (pump0 of new message). On the egress path a transaction is scheduled to an Invalid Destination ID. The egress scheduler sees no shared credits for the Destination. Other transactions to valid Destination IDs makes forward progress. The transaction to invalid Destination ID could still be sent with dedicated credits if available, otherwise will be stuck in the Bridge. The TXN_INFO captures the details of the message in both the casesNote : The illegal HA, CA Slice-ID (MeshID) are not part of the error reporting for the Bridge. Its expected for CA, HA to do the error reporting as applicable. For CA/HA Disable use-models the CA/HA if in clock gated or in reset state and could be silently dropped by the Router or stuck in the Router. Please refer the CA/HA/CMS/Mesh HAS to review the SCF handling in this case 0x001A ubr_error_log.MSCOD26 ubr_error_mask.MSCOD26  22611770187 eeparks 22020679303 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611770193 eeparks 22021152462 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611770195 eeparks 22020679428 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611776210 eeparks Also being seen on IMH1 B0 Cold reset test.  Seems to be only on IMH9. /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/rrnavacu/IMH1_B0/SC_OFF_SVOS_CR_SEQ_0_4T_C4M2_fmods.1/

raw.comments: 22611717822 eeparks 22020679425 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  1667250519 lsamanur Observing common MCA error of ula_mc_st[63:0] in IMH1 v14 as well as IMH2 M5a and M6a models   [mca_monitor.simics] MCA fired: soc_tb.soc.imh9.subfcsouthcap.southcap0.d2d_2.pard2d1uladda1.ula_d2d_1.ula.ulaln_inst.ulacw_inst.ulaln_reg_inst.ulacsr_block.registers.ula_mc_st[63:0]   == 0x8840004502030e0f [mca_monitor.simics] MCA fired: soc_tb.soc.imh9.subfcsouthcap.southcap0.d2d_2.pard2d2uladda0.ula_d2d_0.ula.ulaln_inst.ulacw_inst.ulaln_reg_inst.ulacsr_block.registers.ula_mc_st[63:0]   == 0x8840004402030e0f [mca_monitor.simics] MCA fired: soc_tb.soc.imh8.subfcnorthcap.northcap3.d2d_1.pard2d1uladda0.ula_d2d_0.ula.ulaln_inst.ulacw_inst.ulaln_reg_inst.ulacsr_block.registers.ula_mc_st[63:0]   == 0x8840004202030e0f [mca_monitor.simics] MCA fired: soc_tb.soc.imh8.subfcnorthcap.northcap3.d2d_1.pard2d1uladda1.ula_d2d_1.ula.ulaln_inst.ulacw_inst.ulaln_reg_inst.ulacsr_block.registers.ula_mc_st[63:0]   == 0x8840004302030e0f Model details MODEL dmrmcp-a0-25ww14b_v14.0 dmrmcp2-a0-25ww33b_M5a.0_ici_HSLE dmrmcp2-a0-25ww47a_M6a.0_ici MCA fired ula_mc_st[63:0]   == 0x8840004302030e0f ula_mc_st[63:0]   == 0x8840_0043_0203_0e0f ula_mc_st[63:0]   == 0x8840004302030e0f ula_mc_st register  Field Name Field Access Field Width Field Offset Field Default Present value Field Description mcacod_ll RW/V/P 2 0x0 0 0x3 Bus Interconnect Error LL, Encoding is: 0x0 - Level 0 0x1 - Level 1 0x2 - Level 2 0x3 - Generic LL mcacod_ii RW/V/P 2 0x2 0 0x3 Bus Interconnect Error II, Encoding is: 0x0 - Memory Access 0x1 - Reserved II 0x2 - I/O 0x3 - Other Transaction mcacod_rrrr RW/V/P 4 0x4 0 0x0 Bus Interconnect Error RRRR, Encoding is: 0x0:Generic Error 0x1:Generic Read 0x2:Generic Write 0x3:Data Read 0x4:Data Write 0x5:Instruction Fetch 0x6:Prefetch 0x7:Eviction 0x8:Snoop 0x9-0xF:RESERVED RRRR mcacod_t RW/P 1 0x8 0 0x0 Bus Interconnect Error T, Encoding is: 0:NOTIMEOUT 1:TIMEOUT mcacod_pp RW/V/P 2 0x9 0 0x3   Bus Interconnect Error PP, Encoding is: 0x0 - Local Processor Originated 0x1 - Local Processor Responded 0x2 - Local Processor Observed Error 0x3 - Generic PP mcacod_int RW/V/P 1 0x0B 0 0x1 Bus Interconnect Error, Encoding is: 0 - Not Bus Interconnect Error 1 - Bus Interconnect Error mcacod_rsvd RW/P 4 0x0C 0 0x0 Reserved mscod_code RW/V/P 6 0x10 0 0x3 MSCOD Error Code Error Code. The MSCOD are defined in the HAS. mscod_spare RW/V/P 10 0x16 0 0x8 MSCOD SPARE : Field used for design specific sub-status fields other_info RW/V/P 5 0x20 0 0x3 Other_info maps to the value of the lower 5 bits in ula_straps2.mc_bank_index. When there is a HW error, the ula_straps2.mc_bank_index value will be populated in the other_info field of the Mc_st register, along with the rest of the error status. A SW Write will not update the Mc_st register with the ula_straps2.mc_bank_index value. In certain contexts bank index is referred to as bank instance. The strap value is sampled in ula_straps2.mc_bank_index on the event captured in the HAS fw_upd RW/V/P 1 0x25 0 0x0 Firmware update bit. will be set by BIOS and cleared by hardware whenever MC_status register content will be updated. cor_err_cnt RW/V/P 15 0x26 0 0x1 Corrected Error Count : 15-bit counter which is incremented on every corrected error. MSB (bit 15) is a sticky overflow bit. corr_err_sts_ind RW/V/P 2 0x35 0 0x2 00 - No tracking - No hardware status tracking is provided for the structure reporting this event. 01 - Green - Status tracking is provided for the structure posting the event; the current status is green (below threshold). For more information, see Section ‘Enhanced Cache Error reporting’ of the Software Developer Manual. 10 - Yellow - Status tracking is provided for the structure posting the event; the current status is yellow (below threshold). For more information, see Section ‘Enhanced Cache Error reporting’ of the Software Developer Manual. 11 - Reserved ar RW/P 1 0x37 0 0x0 Indicates (when set) the MCA error code specific recovery action must be performed by system software at the time this error was signaled s RW/P 1 0x38 0 0x0 Signals the reporting of uncorrected, but recoverable (UCR) error. pcc RW/V/P 1 0x39 0 0x0 Indicates (when set) that the state if the processor might have been corrupted by the error condition detected and that reliable restarting of the processor may not be possible. When clear, this flag indicates that the error did not affect the processors state. Software restarting might be possible. addrv RW/V/P 1 0x3A 0 0x0 Address Valid in ULA_MC_AD. Bit set on error types which load Tx Address into ULA_MC_AD. miscv RW/V/P 1 0x3B 0 0x1 MC_MISC valid flag : Indicates (when set) that the MCi_MISC register contains additional information regarding the error. See individual fields of MCi_MISC for details on which are valid for a given error. en RW/V/P 1 0x3C 0 0x0 Error Enable Flag Set by hardware when MCA (uncorrectable) is signaled. uc RW/V/P 1 0x3D 0 0x0 Indicates (when set) that the processor did not or was not able to correct the error condition. When clear, this flag indicates that the processor was able to correct the error condition. over RW/V/P 1 0x3E 0 0x0 MCA Overflow Flag : set on uncorrected second error cases valid RW/V/P 1 0x3F 0 0x1 Set by hardware when an uncorrectable or correctable error is logged.  1667254759 lsamanur The same MCA is seen in both SLE and HSLE environment at different times  In the SLE environment on M6A model MCA occurred after completion of  End of RESET_PHASE_3_INFRA_CFG and start of RESET_PHASE_4 for socket 0  22611737178 eeparks 14023274961 (related-link) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611737220 eeparks Could be related to bugeco:  14023274961 - DMR D2D Stack: UCIE_PHY ww29 drop(side model) - Phy Full training - Timing out in DDA We need to make sure that the following workaround is being applied by PrimeCode or we do it in the reset force scripting.  It also looks like it was fixed in the IP. Workarounds: Primcode write required for below CSR’s after powergood reset. “I_DDA_FDI_LSM_DEFEATURES.timer_en_in_reset_idle_state” & “I_DDA_RDI_USM_DEFEATURES.timer_en_in_reset_idle_state”  1667267031 lsamanur I tried reading back the FDI_LSM & RDI_USM registers but the forced value and read-back values are different. However the timer bit is still disabled. These forces are being during first boot.   Register Forced Value read-back Value Values of timer_en_in_reset_idle_state bit in forced & Readback   I_DDA_FDI_LSM_DEFEATURES 0xa000 0x800a1fc 0 I_DDA_RDI_USM_DEFEATURES 0xb000 0x800b1fc 0 Testbench.log: run area :  /nfs/site/disks/pse_oks_002/lsamanur/DMR/M6A_primecode_WA/logs_hsle/check_primecode_WA.3 As the prints are from first boot, we can see that it is not applied during cold reset. Are they required for cold reset as well?  1667288640 lsamanur I read back the FDI_LSM and RDI_USM registers after a delay and observed that the read-back values matched the forced values. And also we noticed workaround is applied during the first boot. However, when cold reset is issued, even if I apply the timer disable workaround immediately after reset assertion and before the MCA occurs, the issue still exists. Run area :  /nfs/site/disks/pse_oks_002/lsamanur/DMR/M6A_primecode_WA/hsle_logs/check_primecode_WA.0  22611780676 suratako @Shah, Kartik N Please update the latest on this HSD and next steps  1667307099 lsamanur As per Wojciech Kolbusz’s (from primecode team) email so the recommendation is to try with a newer Primecode version. I am currently testing with 2026ww05-4952 Primecode version and will update the ticket once I have results.  1667317481 lsamanur ﻿ MCA is still seen on the SLE environment with the M7 IC model. Reproduced with primecode‑image 2026ww05‑4954, so the issue persists even with the latest image. Run area: /nfs/site/disks/xpg_dmrhub_0554/mohdazee/mcp/emu/a0/regression/AP2_Runs/ic_m7/cold_reset.0  22611801450 eeparks SLE used M7 model, newer version of PrimeCode, and a modified recipe (less forces) to get passed this error.  We will attempt the new recipe on the M7 release with correct PrimeCode to see if we can replicate SLE results.

raw.description: Test Results here:  /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M6a/test_runs/22020413876_CR.1 Cold boot: [    0.000000] Command line: BOOT_IMAGE=/boot/vmlinuz-6.17.0.svos-next-tickless-x86-64 root=LABEL=ROOT ahci_msi=1 console=ttyS0,115200n8 console=tty0 consoleblank=0 earlyprintk=ttyS0,115200 mem=svos@16G pci_msi_enable=1 nokaslr nopti reboot=cold,pci,force intremap=nosid numa=off irqaffinity=0 iommu=pt usb_pm_reset apicmaintimer tsc=reliable lapic=notscdeadline panic=0 tsx=on tdx_host=on tdx_init_rsvd_mem clearcpuid=smap,smep,lass,apx modprobe.blacklist=vmd memmap=0x1000$0xf000 memmap=0x80000$0x40000000 mitigations=off intel_pstate=disable intel_idle.max_cstate=0 idle=poll grub_presi Cold reset: [    0.000000] Command line: BOOT_IMAGE=/boot/vmlinuz-6.17.0.svos-next-tickless-x86-64 root=LABEL=ROOT ahci_msi=1 console=ttyS0,115200n8 console=tty0 consoleblank=0 earlyprintk=ttyS0,115200 kgdboc=kbd,ttyS0 mem=svos@16G pci_msi_enable=1 nokaslr nopti reboot=cold,pci,force intremap=nosid numa=off irqaffinity=0 iommu=pt usb_pm_reset apicmaintimer tsc=reliable lapic=notscdeadline panic=0 tsx=on tdx_host=on tdx_init_rsvd_mem clearcpuid=smap,smep,lass,apx modprobe.blacklist=vmd intel_pstate=disable intel_idle.max_cstate=0 grub_default
raw.subject: sighting
raw.comments: 22611698819 eeparks /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_dcps_001/users/orramire/DMR/MCP/IMH2/M6A/repo/simics_post_script/svos_post.simics   need a while loop in this script branch:   script-branch "SVOS_GRUB_Menu" {     $uart = oakstream_rio.serconsole.con     @SIM_log_info(1,emu.devices,0,"[PPR] Waiting for Press enter to boot the selected OS")     $uart.wait-for-string string = "Press enter to boot the selected OS"     @SIM_log_info(1,emu.devices,0,"[PPR] Selecting the OS version")     $grub_line = 7     while $grub_line {         $uart.input -e "Up"         $grub_line -= 1     }     $uart.input "\015" } Fixed code will look like this: script-branch "SVOS_GRUB_Menu" {     $uart = oakstream_rio.serconsole.con   while(1) {     @SIM_log_info(1,emu.devices,0,"[PPR] Waiting for Press enter to boot the selected OS")     $uart.wait-for-string string = "Press enter to boot the selected OS"     @SIM_log_info(1,emu.devices,0,"[PPR] Selecting the OS version")     $grub_line = 7     while $grub_line {         $uart.input -e "Up"         $grub_line -= 1     }     $uart.input "\015"   } }  22611700423 joshalt confirmed that adding while loop solves the issue. correct kernel is selected through multiple reboots. uart log attached. working on getting it added to M6A PPR

articles.list_title: [Reset][DMR IMH2 MCP HSLE] - ACM failure during boot and reset

raw.title: [Reset][DMR IMH2 MCP HSLE] - ACM failure during boot and reset
raw.description: While running cold and warm reset flows with M5C model, we are observing "LT shutdown" event occurring due to ACM failure.  The ACM failure happens during both boot and reset, but the reset one caused a shutdown in M5C model.  No longer seeing the shutdown in M6A, but still seeing the ACM failure. [image: https://hsdes.intel.com/rest/binary/16029552411] Before it reaches LT shutdown state, there was an MCA and iERR was also followed which was happening around the time of icecode load. [image: https://hsdes.intel.com/rest/binary/16029552412] Cold reset: /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M5c/reset_trs_rio/test_runs/22020413876_CR.0/ Warm reset:   /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M5c/reset_trs_rio/test_runs/22020413864_WR.1   Pcode, Primecode, S3M trackers are available in the same directory. Please note that the LT Shutdown was not observed while running M5A, but the ACM failure was still there: M5A reference logs:  /nfs/site/disks/pse_oks_002/knshah/DMR/MCP_IMH2/RESET/PPR_version/logs/hsle/mcp_ici_hsle_svos_vtc_fmods_cold_reset.5
raw.subject: sighting
raw.sighting_forum_notes: ww05.5 issue not fatal, don't preventing the boot flow. Need investigation why is this timeout happening. also seen in SLE, Hiram to sync with SLE as well WW08.5 Hiram to check with PostSi Security team if they have booted with certain policy in the system configuration where Icecode might casue an LT shutdown with ACM failure

raw.sighting_forum_notes: WW05.5 Similar flow was exercised tried in imh1, only difference is in imh2 using softstraps flow. We'll get the iosf tracker and a trace, also sync with Core team ww08.5 We got a run with Acode 186, to confirm if we are still hitting the CPD timeout in ptracker
raw.comments: 22611728209 eeparks Tracker generation in progress.  22611728210 eeparks 22020679438 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611728211 eeparks 22021152462 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611728212 eeparks Automated Message: This record has been cloned. The preceding comments are originally from the parent record 22022032868.  22611728219 eeparks Also seeing this error for IP disable.  Both of these tests have Soft-straps enabled.  22611728222 eeparks 22022022571 (related-link) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611739645 eeparks 16026002672 (related-link) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611739727 eeparks 13014368516 (related-link) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611739755 eeparks A HW bug was found with a race between S1 or PkgC Entry FSM Acode halt and Acode thermal routine (16026002672, core-pnc-a0-pnc-a0-pm-24ww40a, Nov-24).  A WA was implemented in Acode and Ucode (13012633055, Jan-25).  The WA had a bug and was fixed in Acode rev 177 (13014368516, Jan-26). We have not confirmed that we are hitting this exact issue, but it is likely that the Acode bug exists in this model and we need Acode rev >177 to fix it.  22611739833 eeparks Re-run in the queue with acode tracker enabled.  22611753634 eeparks Trying to get the version of Acode required to test theory that we are hitting a known Acode bug.  22611763384 hmirand @Balasubramanian, Seetharaman @Perez Munoz, Pedro Francisco How do we integrate these fw in the mcp m6a? Ucode /nfs/site/disks/patch_release/DMR/archive_B0/staging/dmr_b0_ucode_26ww03b_IMH1   Acode /p/hdk/rtl/proj_tools/subip_files/core/acode//acode_drop_26WW01_177.0.0_master/drop_link/target/pnc/data_files/  Or you can look in the candidate are (isamba) here: \\isamba.iil.intel.com\nfs\iil\disks\fvcpd_proj_fw\ACODE\PNC\DMR-X\ACODE_DMR_X_ALL_177.0.0_NA_RELEASE\Acode_PNC_SERVER_Collaterals\data_files  22611782491 eeparks We have confirmed that we no longer see the CPD Timeout when running with new Acode.
