raw.title: [DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][SVOS] Reading from unknown MSR. Signaling GP fault.
raw.description: Test run with this issue:   /nfs/site/disks/pse_oks_002/rrnavacu/HSLE/IMH2/M6A/RTC_ESE_29D90_Svos_ColdReset_SEQ_0_4T_C4M2_fmods_vtc.1 Seeing these messages during both boot and reset: [oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[0][0] info] {oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[0][0] 0xffffffff81d1ede9 75622034873089} [20:31:21.0085] 0xc0011029: Reading from unknown MSR. Signaling GP fault. Looking for OS support for why it is trying to access MSRs that don't exist on DMR.
raw.subject: sighting
raw.sighting_forum_notes: ww05.5 Get help from svos team for further analysis. It wasnt seen in previous models (m5a ) WW08.3
raw.comments: 22611729601 eeparks 22020679428 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611729600 eeparks 22020679425 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611729599 eeparks 22020679303 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611729598 knshah Observations: Tried running the setup in NULL mode. This issue is observed in null run as well. On further investigation, found to be a similar issue reported to simics team.  HSD:  16026465047 - [DMR]{CentOS][Perf] List of unknown MSR that Signaling GP fault Observation/Conclusion from the HSD about this MSR: [image: https://hsdes.intel.com/rest/binary/16029644211]  22611729602 eeparks 16026465047 (related-link) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611729603 eeparks 14024592188 (related-link) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611729604 knshah I tried with 6.18 Kernel version (latest SVOS image from 21-Jan-2026). Unfortunately, the issue still exists. logs for reference:  /nfs/site/disks/pse_oks_002/knshah/DMR/A0/HSD_Debug/22021977764/null_run/RTC_ESE_29D90_Svos_ColdReset_SEQ_0_4T_C4M2_fmods_vtc.9 I will reach out to SVOS kernel support person to get details about the same.  22611729605 acpreble So are these MSRs MSR_ATOM_CORE_RATIOS and MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG? They look to be very fundamental. I think the definition for it goes back to 2017 in arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h. Is there some reason those are not implemented in Simics? That message is Simics saying it's going to cause a #GP because it's not recognizing those MSRs. So the kernel just winds up having to react to the fault. Are you trying to get a special kernel that has lobotomized out code that's trying to use that stuff? You'd presumably have to strip out all the power management code. I wouldn't know what kernel build configs could do that (if they even can). That would have to be an iterative process and I'd eventually probably have to just hand off a strategy for that to you to refine if you really need a kernel that doesn't touch those MSRs.  22611729606 knshah Hi @Preble, Adam C , Thanks for your inputs. In this HSD, we were looking more about 0xc0011029 MSR which is resulting in GP Fault.  We have 2 questions (if you can help us to get the answers): 1. Why AMD-specific MSR is being accessed by kernel? 2. Is there a way to blacklist/skip this specific MSR getting accessed at all? Some more details about 0xc0011029 MSR:  [image: https://hsdes.intel.com/rest/binary/16029677885]  22611729607 acpreble A filtered grep in 6.18 for that MSR: arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG               0xc0011029 arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT   1 arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE      BIT_ULL(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT) arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_ZEN2_FP_BACKUP_FIX_BIT 9 arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c:      msr_set_bit(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, 31); arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c:              msr_set_bit(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_ZEN2_FP_BACKUP_FIX_BIT); arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c:              msr_clear_bit(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_ZEN2_FP_BACKUP_FIX_BIT); arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c:              msr_set_bit(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c:                          MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT); arch/x86/kernel/cpu/hygon.c:            msr_set_bit(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, arch/x86/kernel/cpu/hygon.c:                        MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT); arch/x86/kvm/svm/svm.c: case MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG: arch/x86/kvm/svm/svm.c:                 *data |= MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE; arch/x86/kvm/svm/svm.c: case MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG: arch/x86/kvm/svm/svm.c: case MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG: { arch/x86/kvm/x86.c:     MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG, arch/x86/power/cpu.c:           { MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG,      X86_FEATURE_LFENCE_RDTSC }, tools/arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG         0xc0011029 tools/arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT     1 tools/arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE        BIT_ULL(MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_LFENCE_SERIALIZE_BIT) tools/arch/x86/include/asm/msr-index.h:#define MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG_ZEN2_FP_BACKUP_FIX_BIT 9 tools/testing/selftests/kvm/x86/feature_msrs_test.c:    return msr != MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG; The places where I see it straight up touching that MSR are all in arch/x86/kernel/cpu/amd.c. I would think if it's getting that far, the kernel decided you're running an AMD processor. I'm guessing you're not hehehe. My first bit would be to skim dmesg and see in the startup if it's declaring the CPU (it think) it's using. If it's screwing that up then everybody gets to figure out if Simics isn't reporting it properly and/or the kernel's not detecting it properly.  22611729608 knshah Strange to see that the artifactory for SVOS image contains the name "amd64" in its naming convention! Path:   Index of simics-repos/ts/images/dmr/svos/202601261346 [image: https://hsdes.intel.com/rest/binary/16029680347] I can see that we do not have any connection with AMD64 w.r.t. cores in the system. [image: https://hsdes.intel.com/rest/binary/16029680348]  22611729609 acpreble Debian is using AMD64 to representing the 64-bit x86-based architecture. They've been doing that since it was first introduced as a package target back in 2007. It has nothing to do with targeting specifically AMD processors.  22611729610 eeparks Automated Message: This record has been cloned. The preceding comments are originally from the parent record 22021977982.  22611778602 eeparks Hi,   I started debugging this issue to get an idea of what might be causing it. The first thing I found is that this error is not exclusive to svos-next, the exact same message appears with the Linux 6.1.0-42 kernel (which is included in the SVOS images for recovery).   This error always occurs after this point in the kernel messages:   [    8.837277] NET: Registered PF_PACKET protocol family [    8.839041] mpls_gso: MPLS GSO support [    8.845486] microcode: sig=0x400f11, pf=0x80, revision=0x52000202   Checking the assembly code being executed, it clearly corresponds to the Linux kernel context:   [oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[1][0]] cs:0xffffffff81aafb36 p:0x0000002eafb36  mov dword ptr [rbx+0x8],ecx [oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[1][0]] cs:0xffffffff81aafb39 p:0x0000002eafb39  rdmsr [oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[1][0] info] 0xc0011029: Reading from unknown MSR. Signaling GP fault.   Here, the MSR being read is obtained from a memory region (further debugging is required to identify which code sets this MSR value).   The kernel function executing this code is __pfx_msr_build_context.constprop.0:   84819 ffffffff81aafa00 t __pfx_bsp_pm_callback 84820 ffffffff81aafa10 t bsp_pm_callback 84821 ffffffff81aafa70 t __pfx_msr_build_context.constprop.0 84822 ffffffff81aafa80 t msr_build_context.constprop.0 84823 ffffffff81aafbd0 t __pfx_pm_check_save_msr 84824 ffffffff81aafbe0 t pm_check_save_msr   And this function is only called from the code in power/cpu.c:   arch/x86/power/cpu.c:static int msr_build_context(const u32 *msr_id, const int num) arch/x86/power/cpu.c:   return msr_build_context(bdw_msr_id, ARRAY_SIZE(bdw_msr_id)); arch/x86/power/cpu.c:   return msr_build_context(cpuid_msr_id, ARRAY_SIZE(cpuid_msr_id)); arch/x86/power/cpu.c:                   msr_build_context(&msr_enum[i].msr_no, 1);   So, it seems that this incorrect MSR read falls within the power management area of the Linux kernel.   Saludos/Regards Roberto Cervantes  22611780955 eeparks e-mail from Adam Preble: Of the hits in there, I would bet on the msr_build_context call in pm_save_spec_msr (arch/x86/power/cpu.c) since that’s invoked during device init. That would line up with happening during boot. The code:   static void pm_save_spec_msr(void) {                 struct msr_enumeration {                                 u32 msr_no;                                 u32 feature;                 } msr_enum[] = {                                 { MSR_IA32_SPEC_CTRL,              X86_FEATURE_MSR_SPEC_CTRL },                                 { MSR_IA32_TSX_CTRL, X86_FEATURE_MSR_TSX_CTRL },                                 { MSR_TSX_FORCE_ABORT,         X86_FEATURE_TSX_FORCE_ABORT },                                 { MSR_IA32_MCU_OPT_CTRL, X86_FEATURE_SRBDS_CTRL },                                 { MSR_AMD64_LS_CFG, X86_FEATURE_LS_CFG_SSBD },                                 { MSR_AMD64_DE_CFG,              X86_FEATURE_LFENCE_RDTSC },                 };                 int i;                   for (i = 0; i < ARRAY_SIZE(msr_enum); i++) {                                 if (boot_cpu_has(msr_enum[i].feature))                                                 msr_build_context(&msr_enum[i].msr_no, 1);                 } }   Well, there are some AMD MSRs in there. I would guess that the CPU is claiming to have at least one of those two features that would instigate an AMD MSR read:   X86_FEATURE_LS_CFG_SSBD X86_FEATURE_LFENCE_RDTSC   arch/x86/include/asm/cpufeatures.h:#define X86_FEATURE_LS_CFG_SSBD              ( 7*32+24)  /* AMD SSBD implementation via LS_CFG MSR */ arch/x86/include/asm/cpufeatures.h:#define X86_FEATURE_LFENCE_RDTSC     (20*32+ 2) /* LFENCE always serializing / synchronizes RDTSC */   Which correspond to decimal indices: 248 642   One way to grab the kernel by the face and tell it you absolutely, positively don’t have those features is the clearcpuid kernel boot parameter. So when booting into grub (or just set this in the files in /etc/svos/params.d):   clearcpuid=248,642   (If there’s already a clearcpuid parameter, just tack those fields on, separated by commas)   If that magically works, then you have to figure out what decided the enable those bits before the kernel ever saw them.  1667309469 amadaan After updating the GRUB configuration with clearcpuid=248,642. The system boots successfully without this "0xc0011029: Reading from unknown MSR" issue. The cleared CPUID bits appear to have resolved the MSR access issue. Without clearcpuid update: After clearcpuid update:  14615114236 acpreble It sounds at this point like you want to take that upstream and see if the model or BIOS have something goofy going on. I can't remember how this has gone in the past so I don't have more to add after that.

raw.title: [DMR][HIOP][MCP HSLE] Emulation cycles stop advancing after pciextor/VTC receives poisoned write from p2p packet
raw.description: We are hitting an issue where after we send a poisoned write to the VTCs, the emulation cycles stop advancing and the run eventually hits a timeout. Within the VTC we set  ERRMSK and ERRSEV to ignore the poison error but still encounter the sa me problem, so we are thinking that the pcie xtor is misbehaving after receiving poison. Interesting note, when we run this same test in MIO model, we don't see any issues. Run  - /nfs/site/disks/pse_dmr_snv_004/users/qata/imh2/mcp_multistack/m5c/14024453913_p2p_poison_nopcieinj_tunneled.2   #from testbench, you see the rest of the run polling for this mop completion and the emulation cycles never move forward [emu.devices.bifurcator_s0_imh9_dstack_1.pcie_xtors[0] info] {emu.xtorgroup_pcie_s0_imh9_dstack_1.engine 0x1f309f19 8372482441} [16:37:47.8714] PCIe:   8372482378 Error is ERR_RVD_POISON num_extra_dword 1 <oakstream_rio.serconsole.con>MmEntryNotifyProtocolCallback () : Triggered.\r\n [emu.devices.lcd_s0_imh9_dstack_1_x16a.cambria_error_detection info] {emu.xtorgroup_pcie_s0_imh9_dstack_1.engine 0x1f309f19 8372482441} [16:37:47.8739] Error Detected type 47 ERR_RVD_POISON - TRANS_LYR [emu.devices.lcd_s0_imh9_dstack_1_x16a.cambria_error_detection info] {emu.xtorgroup_pcie_s0_imh9_dstack_1.engine 0x1f309f19 8372482441} [16:37:47.8746] Write successful in Func0 Error Register [emu.devices.lcd_s0_imh9_dstack_1_x16a.cambria_error_detection info] {emu.xtorgroup_pcie_s0_imh9_dstack_1.engine 0x1f309f19 8372482441} [16:37:47.8751] Write successful in Func0 Error Register [emu.devices.bifurcator_s0_imh9_dstack_1.pcie_xtors[0] info] {emu.xtorgroup_pcie_s0_imh9_dstack_1.engine 0x1f309f19 8372482441} [16:37:47.8757] PCIe: target_bus_op write to 0x154240000034 poisoned and target is unmodified, returning Sim_PE_IO_Not_Taken [idi_hybrid_comp_s0_cbb0_Core0.oakstream_rio_mb_mcp0_cbb_die0_tile0_module0_core00_blocking_bridge_io info] {oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[0][0] 0x7d2a98fd 112917033510642} [16:37:53.0042]  Waiting for  mop addr = 0x504 , size = 0x4 , op_type = read  cycle : (start 8372482441 , current 8372482441 ) , tag 105 [idi_hybrid_comp_s0_cbb0_Core0.oakstream_rio_mb_mcp0_cbb_die0_tile0_module0_core00_blocking_bridge_io info] {oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[0][0] 0x7d2a98fd 112917033510642} [16:37:53.0044] please wait for mop to finish or run interrupt_blocking_bridge.sh from shadow dir to interrupt [idi_hybrid_comp_s0_cbb0_Core0.oakstream_rio_mb_mcp0_cbb_die0_tile0_module0_core00_blocking_bridge_io info] {oakstream_rio.mb.mcp0.cbb_die0.tile0.module0.core[0][0] 0x7d2a98fd 112917033510642} [16:37:59.0041]   Waiting for  mop addr = 0x504 , size = 0x4 , op_type = read  cycle : (start 8372482441 , current 8372482441 ) , tag 105

raw.title: [Pre-sighting->Sighting][DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][SVOS] Punit Machine Check Error and IERR not propagating during Reset Flow
raw.description: Test run area:   /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M6a/test_runs/22021152462_AWR.2 The Punit Machine Check Error does not survive the reset flow and the IERR does not propagate to the SOC during the reset flow.  The IERR does not trigger and is not seen in the testbench.log file. This test has soft-straps enabled, so a cold reset happens during BIOS.  The error that happens during cold reset is a UDI ResetPrep timeout ( 22021912430 - [DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][M5C][M6A][SVOS] Cold & Warm Reset is hitting the RESETPREP_TIMEOUT in CBB due to lack of UDI response ) Cold Reset (mca_trk_socket0_cbb0.log): Time        | core | cNCU | sNCU                                     | Punit raw.title: [DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][SVOS] Punit Machine Check Error and IERR not propagating during Reset Flow
raw.description: Test run area:   /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M6a/test_runs/22021152462_AWR.2 The Punit Machine Check Error does not survive the reset flow and the IERR does not propagate to the SOC during the reset flow.  The IERR does not trigger and is not seen in the testbench.log file. This test has soft-straps enabled, so a cold reset happens during BIOS.  The error that happens during cold reset is a UDI ResetPrep timeout ( 22021912430 - [DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][M5C][M6A][SVOS] Cold & Warm Reset is hitting the RESETPREP_TIMEOUT in CBB due to lack of UDI response ) Cold Reset (mca_trk_socket0_cbb0.log): Time        | core | cNCU | sNCU                                     | Punit The preceding comments are originally from the parent record 22022059662.  14615066754 eeparks The root RASIP is getting RESET_PREP prior to when the Punit flags the error: 3760181761 |       gpsbep__punit_punit_iosf_sb_agent_gpsb_messages_imh_0_fsa_north__punit_punit_iosf_sb_agent_gpsb_messages |   P | (0012)                 PUNIT_PUNIT_IOSF_SB_AGENT_GPSB_MESSAGES_PUNIT_FSM | (003B)                            ROOT_RAS_RASIP_SB_AGENT_RASIP_SB_AGENT |      |               (2A) RESETPREP The infra RASIP is also getting RESET_PREP prior to when the Punit flags the error: 3760192281 |       gpsbep__punit_punit_iosf_sb_agent_gpsb_messages_imh_0_fsa_north__punit_punit_iosf_sb_agent_gpsb_messages |   P | (0012)                 PUNIT_PUNIT_IOSF_SB_AGENT_GPSB_MESSAGES_PUNIT_FSM | (003C)                           INFRA_RAS_RASIP_SB_AGENT_RASIP_SB_AGENT |      |               (2A) RESETPREP  Punit is asserting YY_IERR_TX: 3814943344  |      |      |                                          |                             |                                                |       |     | YY_IERR_TX=0x1  Reset and boot High-Level RAS Reset Requirements: S0 –Post S0 “All resets” shall be held off during error harvesting flows pre-reset or post-reset All resets => HW generated resets, HPRTO, OS generated One stop mechanism within SoC to hold resets off when error harvesting is enabled with no platform involvement OOB path shall remain available for pre-reset harvesting All sticky registers/state is preserved in IPs, post warm reset (any flavor of WR, graceful or ungraceful). Sticky registers shall be cleared on cold reset. Reset entry/exit shall be clean despite the corrupt/error state. Reset exit is clean so that system is stable enough for post-reset harvesting via BIOS or OOB Error harvesting flow/entity shall be in control of warm reset or global reset All fatal errors in ‘reset flow’ should be routed to CATERR Fatal errors result in assertion of ‘surprise’ reset by the platform following pre-reset error harvesting/crashlog collection. Note: IPs should not send Machine Check errors prior to config_done (phase 5) in reset; this is because the RASIP will drop the MCA_MSG and not signal the error on CATERR# - this will result in a loss of error logging and signaling.  22611753937 eeparks Trace captured around the Punit IERR for the AWR.  YY_IERR_TX is visible in IMH8 and triggers ierr_aggr3 seen by Punit.  RASIP does not seem to be aware of IERR and rasip_ierr_cbb0_rx is dumped as constant 0.  root_1_rasip_configdone is 0 as well.  22611763416 hmirand AR Edwin:  Look at timing of RESET_PREP to RASIP and XX_RESET assertion.  There will be a time where a CBB error could matter and not be serviced. Also, Bentley, Jonathan B and Pugaonkar, Shubhada (RAS WG) will discuss it to bring it to the RAS WG and discuss the architecture  22611764265 eeparks 3822190075 - CBB Punit GoS1 3822507935 - CBB Punit CPD Enter 3824945975 - CBB Punit Wait Reset_Prep 3826632035 - CBB Punit Wait XX_RESET 3833455321 - IMH Root RASIP ResetPrepAck 3833458235 - CBB Punit is still doing stuff before and after RASIP ResetPrepAck (Palpha, sync_tx, HWPResolve, ProbeModeHandler, PeriodicRegisterUpdate::activity_os_reporting_tx, WorkpointCalc::wp_calc_launcher_tx, RingDomain::handle_ccf_dvfs_done_tx, CbbCstateFsm::cbb_cstate_fsm_tx, UCIe::handle_d2d_dvfs_done_tx, AutonomousPstate::update_configuration_tx) 3836611923 - IMH PLTRST 3836712323 - XX_RESET Asserted by platform 3836715275 - CBB Punit MEM_R [0x60000850]       8b  xxreset_assert_event 3836718035 - CBB Punit Last PkgSFsmState read of Wait XX_Reset 3855783835 - CBB Punit PhaseE Start 3856130555 - CBB Punit CR_W  [    0x9284]           RESET_ENTRY_CONTROL  - BOOTFSM_RESET_ENTRY_TRIGGER=1 (last thing Punit does during reset) 5116964835 - CBB Punit S0 achieved  22611774253 hmirand PostSi team also seeing in on IMH1 https://hsdes.intel.com/appstore/article-one/#/16029808426 https://hsdes.intel.com/appstore/article-one/#/14027017329  22611776988 eeparks Posed a question to AI and got this answer: Examples of Hardware Errors During Reset Entry Flow During the reset entry flow, certain hardware errors can trigger IERR, including: Fatal errors in the PLL, FIVR, or Bandgap (critical infrastructure IPs). These may also assert MON_FAIL, which is designed to provide robust error indication during early reset stages. Fatal errors detected by the Punit, Pcode, CCF, or Core blocks, such as: Double-bit ECC errors in internal memories Uncorrectable parity errors in fabric or interconnects Power management controller failures Severe clock or voltage instability detected by infrastructure monitors If such an error occurs during reset entry, the CBB will: Put Pcode into an error loop or freeze state (stopping active power management, but hardware protectors like PMAX and PROCHOT remain functional). Trigger the Crash Log flow to capture error context. Notify the RAS IP (Reliability, Availability, Serviceability Intellectual Property) and propagate the IERR signal as needed. The highlighted section is what we are talking about here.  Any error during reset entry will not fulfill this requirement, including fatal hardware errors on a degrading part.  The error might not be fatal to the flow itself but could be an indicator of something else going bad in the CBB that we would want to be aware of and eventually debug.  Detecting and triggering data collection will be difficult without a mechanism to propagate the IERR signal out to the platform and start a crashlog flow.

raw.description: test results here:   /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M6a/test_runs/22020413876_CR.1 In Cold reset case, MCA is present but it does not hang, its able to boot UEFI Also present in this run:  /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M6a/test_runs/22021152462_AWR.2/ In AWR scenario we see this mca and later it hangs [emu.devices info] {emu.engine 0x20d536e8 8813506156} [12:44:41.7135] [reset_phase_marker.simics] End of RESET_PHASE_7 for socket 0 [emu.devices info] {emu.engine 0x20d536e8 8813506156} [12:44:41.7143] [reset_phase_marker.simics] END_OF_BIOS [emu.devices info] {emu.engine 0x20d536e8 8813506156} [12:44:41.7155] [8,813,506,156] [bios_fetchor_control.simics] loading test mem $test8bit_mem into bios fetchor [8813512959] test_end_checker: Compute0M31C1 sleep reason = 0 [8813539639] test_end_checker: Compute0M31C0 sleep reason = 0 [mca_monitor.simics] MCA fired: soc_tb.soc.imh9.subfcsouthcap.southcap0.paridfttop.root_rasip.rasip.i_rasip_registers.rasip_regs_inst.registers.reg_CR_MCI_STATUS[63:0]   cycle count = 8813557003 [mca_monitor.simics] MCA fired: soc_tb.soc.imh9.subfcsouthcap.southcap0.paridfttop.root_rasip.rasip.i_rasip_registers.rasip_regs_inst.registers.reg_CR_MCI_STATUS[63:0]   == 0xee000000001a0412 RST_TAG doing the procedure for core switch From:  https://docs.intel.com/documents/iparch/scf/HAS/Gen4/HAS/BRIDGE/Latest/HAS/SCF_GEN4_LATEST_BRIDGE_HAS.html MCACOD Specifies the machine-check architecture-defined error code for the machine-check error condition detected. The machine-check architecture defined error codes are guaranteed to be the same for all IA-32 processors that implement the machine-check architecture. For Bridge this field is always marked as 0x412 in the Full Bank MCA message Error Codes MCACOD must be 0x412 for Bridge invalid_meshid This error is flagged for the below 2 cases. The ‘invalid’ ID is based on the NIP_ *SUPPORTED_ MESHID parameter, ie; NIP_ SUPPORTED_ MESH_ ID for Non-P2P and NIP_ P2P_ SUPPORTED_ MESH_ ID for P2P transactions. Received a transaction from NIP with an invalid Source MeshID . The packet is dropped and credits are not returned as the Source ID is invalid. For BL messages, the 2nd pump could trigger another error as it will be considered as a new message (pump0 of new message). On the egress path a transaction is scheduled to an Invalid Destination ID . The egress scheduler sees no shared credits for the Destination. Other transactions to valid Destination IDs makes forward progress. The transaction to invalid Destination ID could still be sent with dedicated credits if available, otherwise will be stuck in the Bridge. The TXN_ INFO captures the details of the message in both the cases Note : The illegal HA, CA Slice-ID (MeshID) are not part of the error reporting for the Bridge. Its expected for CA, HA to do the error reporting as applicable. For CA/HA Disable use-models the CA/HA if in clock gated or in reset state and could be silently dropped by the Router or stuck in the Router. Please refer the CA/HA/CMS/Mesh HAS to review the SCF handling in this case 0x001A ubr_ error_ log.MSCOD26 ubr_ error_ mask.MSCOD26 test results here:   /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M6a/test_runs/22020679437_AGR.0 [mca_monitor.simics] MCA fired: soc_tb.soc.imh9.subfcsouthcap.southcap0.paridfttop.root_rasip.rasip.i_rasip_registers.rasip_regs_inst.registers.reg_CR_MCI_STATUS[63:0]   cycle count = 5661186363 [mca_monitor.simics] MCA fired: soc_tb.soc.imh9.subfcsouthcap.southcap0.paridfttop.root_rasip.rasip.i_rasip_registers.rasip_regs_inst.registers.reg_CR_MCI_STATUS[63:0]   == 0xee000000001a0412 [mca_monitor.simics] MISCV is 1 [mca_monitor.simics] MCA fired: soc_tb.soc.imh9.subfcsouthcap.southcap0.paridfttop.root_rasip.rasip.i_rasip_registers.rasip_regs_inst.registers.reg_CR_MCI_MISC[63:0]   == 0xc0f0030906075686 [mca_monitor.simics] ADDRV is 1 [mca_monitor.simics] MCA fired: soc_tb.soc.imh9.subfcsouthcap.southcap0.paridfttop.root_rasip.rasip.i_rasip_registers.rasip_regs_inst.registers.reg_CR_MCI_ADDR[63:0]   == 0x8c000

raw.sighting_forum_notes:
WW09.5 Might be related to same issue  









 
 
 
 
 
 22022100537 
 [DMR][Reset][AP1B0][AP2][MCP][SVOS]
 IMH9 RASIP MCA after Cold Reset 0xee000000001a0412 
 
 
 try commenting out the two lines that load the test code in the below script: input_dir/simics_scripts/bios_fetchor_control.simics 159:        #@log_bios_fetchor("loading test mem $test8bit_mem into bios fetchor")             178:        #@log_bios_fetchor("loading test mem $test8bit_mem into bios fetchor")
raw.comments: 22611778893 eeparks Automated Message: This record has been cloned. The preceding comments are originally from the parent record 22022105765.  22611778920 eeparks 22020679437 (child-parent) - link(s) are added via link tab.  22611817550 eeparks Information from FIVR bugeco: FIVR is alerting correctly as the indication that the bg_good signal went down indicating that the bgref is invalid while fivr is still regulating. traced the signal to a register write happening on the register driving the bg_good signal. 2 issues here.  1) that could be a stimulus thing,  Vccinf_powergood should be the 1st to go down when the SOC is shutting down and not bg_powergood 2) and that is more concerning:  we could not find any connection between the output of the bgr ( bgr_powergood) and the input fo the fivr ( bgr_powergood) this needs to be investigated  in normal mode if the bgr failed the fivr needs to see that and shutdown its voltage.  not sure how this flow is implemented in this case  22611818272 eeparks BIOS does report the error after the AGR flow completes: [   10.848472] BERT: [Hardware Error]: Skipped 1 error records [   10.849812] BERT: Total records found: 1

raw.title: [DMR][Reset][Warm][IP Disable][VTC][MCP] Punit MCA after triggering reset from OS 0xa800000083000402 - RESETPREP timeout on enabled stacks
raw.description:
Boot with some IP disable through bios knobs After the 1st boot successfully , change the bios knobs in OS and issue a reset Fails after issuing warm and cold reset Knobs from 1st boot: StackDisableBitMap_0_Bits_Fio1=1 
StackDisableBitMap_0_Bits_Fio2=1 
StackDisableBitMap_0_Bits_Fio3=1 
StackDisableBitMap_0_Bits_Fio11=1 
StackDisableBitMap_0_Bits_Fio12=1 Knobs after the reset: Restore the previous knobs to enable rundir :  IMH2: | 876305645.0 ns |  RBID_MIO_3_RAS              |  IP_DISABLE     |  0x001  |   1   |   1   |       |       | | 876306285.0 ns |  RBID_MIO_2_RAS              |  IP_DISABLE     |  0x001  |   1   |   1 0x001  |   1   |   0   |       |       |  14615235435 eeparks Looking at the HWRS_S0_D8_RSTBUS_TRK.txt, we found that the RBID values in /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_cit_015/users/agudinog/dmrmcp2-a0-25ww47a_M6a.0_ici/subip/sip/hwrs/src/rtl/common/hwrs_rstw_portid_groupid_defines.sv does not match what is in the RTL (strap_port_id from the trace). So, using the correct portID from the RTL we get the following IP_DISABLES sent: IMH8:  MIO_0_RAS (0x448), MIO_3_RAS (0x54B), MIO_2_RAS (0x548) IMH9:  MIO_1_RAS (0x4C8), MIO_2_RAS (0x548) We can see that the HW is actually disabled in the trace, so these RBID numbers were sent disables when they should not have done.  MIO_0_RAS was sent the IP_DISABLE message, so won't respond to RESETPREP on IMH8.  MIO_0_RAS was not sent IP_DISABLE on IMH9, so it did respond to RESETPREP.  MIO_1_RAS was sent IP_DISABLE, so will also not respond to RESETPREP.  22611813941 hmirand Snippet from the  trace  22611816470 eeparks Confirmed that we are NOT seeing this error on IMH1B0.  The error on IMH1B0 was from a left-over workaround that no longer applies (MMC RESETPREP workaround). Here is a run that does not have the error: /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/joshalt/MCP/IMH1_B0/test_runs/VTC_IP_Enable_Disable.4  22611840780 eeparks Confirmed that updated PrimeCode with changed mapping does not experience this error and we can execute the flow. IP enable after disable passed in this run - /nfs/site/disks/pse_oks_002/arvindnr/DMR/M6A/logs/ipenable_disable_cold_mcp_ici_hsle_svos_vtc_fmods.3 Pass / Fail criteria: -Check that cold reset is triggered by CPU SS provisioning. -Check that the IO stack instances disabled during 1st boot are enabled during the 2nd boot as seen in the BIOS UART log. Left is 1st boot. Right is 2nd boot.  22611840787 hmirand 0. What should DCCB know? This bug was caused by a mismapping of RSTW RBID values to HWRS macros for UIO RAS. 1. What bad thing happens When the UIO stacks are disabled via soft straps/bios, the wrong RAS IP gets disabled due to the bad mapping and primecode never gets RESETPREP returned causing an MCA. 2. What are the impacted projects? (i.e list all projects impacted since the first project that has exposure to this issue) COR IMH, COR IOH, DMR IMH 3. Recommend how to address for each release (list specifics for every project/stepping including SI: fix, reject, or workaround) DMR IMH2: Primecode WA COR IMH/IOH: Fix Macro Mapping. 4. Propose if this bug will gate PRQ. No since primecode WA is feasible. 5. Will this bug require an Errata? No 6. Is this bug ecoable? No. 7. Give a brief logical description of the changes needed Update the HWRS macro mapping and respin hwrs, or attempt eco of strap values on the rstws themselves. 8. Describe the possible workarounds & feasibility Primecode will update its internal mapping of RBIDs to RSTWs so that when it issues IP disables on a per subip basis, it will send the disable to the correct RAS RSTW for the associated UIO. Confirmed feasible with primecode team. 9. Review with and describe the impact to End Users (SV, OEM) None 10. Review with and describe the impact to SW/FW: Update to primecode mapping needed. 11. Describe the impact to Schedules: None if WA pursued. 12. List the IP interface/global signal changes 13. Describe any risk created by fixing 14. What is the impact to Clocks or Reset: Causes issues with warm reset exit when using BIOS driven stack disables. 15. What is the gate count and timing impact:  None 16. List Partition changes: parhwrsfuse 17. What validation is required for the fix (Pre-si, Post-Si, SLE, FPGA etc): Emulation 18. Why was the issue found now: Pre-Si val doesn't run with primecode. 19. Has the issue been reviewed with arch: Yes, with Chih-Cheh 20. Is there power impact? If yes, has it been signed off by the power team? No power impact.

raw.title: [DMR][Reset][AP2][MCP][SVOS] IMH9 hangs waiting for FEATURE_IP_RESET_COMPLETE in Warm Reset exit after Soft-Straps Cold Reset
raw.description:
Hitting this hang in several tests that perform a warm reset after a soft-straps cold reset.  Both M6a and M7 are seeing this issue. SWR, AWR, and WR flows are all affected. SWR in XOS: /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M6a/XOS/test_runs/22021505439_XOS_SWR_Traffic.1 AWR hang is slightly different, in that IMH8 hangs at this point: /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M6a/test_runs/22021152462_AWR.1 M7 WR test example: /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M7/test_runs/M7_cxl_pcie_WR.1 [emu_mca_mon]
MCA fired:
soc_tb.soc.imh9.subfcsouthcap.southcap1.parpunit.punit.ras.cr_neb_gen.emu_mca_mon_inst   cycle count = 13636449983 

 [emu_mca_mon]
MCA fired:
soc_tb.soc.imh9.subfcsouthcap.southcap1.parpunit.punit.ras.cr_neb_gen.emu_mca_mon_inst   == 0xA80000007C000402 I did have a WR run that got through this flow on M7: /nfs/site/disks/ive_dmr_prednv_008/eeparks/MCP_HSLE/M7/test_runs/M7_cxl_pcie_WR.2 The M7 version h angs
at:   imh primecode state CONFIGURE_HPM
